0: <title>Screening LCA of French organic amendments and fertilisers </title>
Received: 3 July 2019 / Accepted: 16 January 2020 / Published online: 4 February 2020 Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2020
1: <header>Abstract </header>

Introduction In France, agricultural recycling of organic waste is widespread, but LCIs of organic waste treatments are scarce. This work presents LCIs of the most relevant organic waste treatments yielding residual organic amendments and fertilisers, and organic residue-based industrial processes yielding commercial organic amendments and fertilisers, in France. It also presents the results from a screening LCA performed on these LCIs.

Material and methods LCIs were built from mainly secondary data sources. Four functional units were retained: 1 t fresh mass of final product and 1 t of N, P, or K in fresh mass. Economic allocation was applied, following the French Environment and Energy Management Agency position. The International Reference Life Cycle Data System 2011 suit for impact assessment was retained, and all impacts were expressed as single scores to facilitate comparisons. Organic wastes considered were liquid and solid manures, agro-industrial residues, sewage sludge, green wastes and harvest residues, and biowaste. Treatment steps modelled were stocking, shredding, sieving, and mixing of substrates; anaerobic and aerobic treatments; thickening, dewatering, drying, and pelletising; various biological and physicochemical treatments of phase-separated liquid fractions; and the industrial production of organic amendments and fertilisers. Comparisons were performed among products classified as amendments or fertilisers.

Results and discussion LCIs of typical treatment routes are presented, complemented with screening life cycle impact assessment results. Dried sludge and treated solid fractions of agricultural digestates present consistently higher impacts than any other amendment, mainly due to their associated energy use, relatively low N contents, and the use of maize silage as input. These items contribute in average > 50% of the aggregated impacts. Untreated manure and slurry feature the lowest relative impacts for all functional units. Compost-based commercial organic amendments have similar impacts to other composts per t of fresh mass, but slightly lower per t of nutrients or C, due to the inclusion of additional nutrient-rich inputs. Regarding fertilisers, the impact intensity of commercial organo-mineral fertilisers and treated liquid fractions of slurry and agricultural digestate is within similar orders of magnitude. Manure-based digestates feature relatively low impacts per all functional units. Only some digestates and untreated poultry manure feature impacts similar to those of N mineral fertilisers.

Conclusions Energy provision and direct emissions are the main contributors to impacts in organic waste treatments. Modelling choices such as the allocation of impacts to agricultural by-products heavily influence specific impact categories, mainly climate change. Overall, these results represent a needed contribution to background data used in agricultural LCA.
Keywords AGRIBALYSE Compost . Digestate . Organic fertiliser . Sewage sludge
Editorial Responsibility: Ian Vázquez-Rowe

5: <header>1 Introduction </header>

Population growth, in combination with increasing urbanisa- tion, is generating large amounts of wastes (i.e. unwanted or unusable material, substances, or by-products), including or- ganic residues (i.e. fermentable/putrescible materials), whose management often represents a non-negligible cost factor. Simultaneously, agriculture demands increasing volumes of fertilising inputs, in a context of a rising global interest in more sustainable food production (Foster et al. 2006; Muller et al. 2017), but also of a rising food security challenge in many developing countries (Ingram et al. 2010; Godfray et al.

2012). In line with the increasingly popular concepts of Circular Economy and Green Economy (EC 2014; Loiseau et al. 2016), organic residues such as green waste, biowaste, wastewater treatment sludge, manures, and agricultural and agro-industrial residues are increasingly being recycled in ag- ricultural soils, often treated for hygienisation or concentra- tion, and/or otherwise rendered into organic-sourced fertiliser products (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Brockmann et al. 2018).

For environmental assessment, the ISO-standardised (ISO 2006) life cycle assessment (LCA) framework is widely used, due to its comprehensiveness (multiple impact categories) and flexibility, which allow the type of modelling adapted to rep- resent the key material and substance flows required for mean- ingful comparisons. Various methodological challenges re- main in agricultural LCA, especially when the goal includes system comparisons. One key challenge is the selection of a baseline system for comparison, given the fact that legislation forces the treatment of certain organic wastes before disposal, and that if waste disposal is included in the system, the func- tion delivered by the said system is expanded into fertilisation + waste disposal. Other standing challenges include the selec- tion of the right functional unit (Ponsioen and van der Werf 2015; Notarnicola et al. 2017), the allocation of impacts among co-products-including manure and crop residues- (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner 2015; Chen et al. 2016; Mackenzie et al. 2016), the consideration of contaminants transferred to soil (Bernstad and la Cour 2012; Laurent et al. 2014; Harder et al. 2015; Zang et al. 2015), the modelling of direct field emissions (Bockstaller et al. 2008, 2015), the com- putation of mineral fertiliser equivalents of organic fertilisers (Brockmann et al. 2018; Stedje et al. 2018), the consideration of soil quality changes (Benoist and Bessou 2018), and the availability of background life cycle data for modelling fertilisation. To deal with the latter, various national and inter- national agricultural life cycle inventory (LCI) databases have and are being built, such as ecoinvent (Nemecek and Kagi 2007; Nemecek and Schnetzer 2012), World Food LCA Database (Nemecek et al. 2014), Agri-footprint (Blonk Agri- footprint 2014) and AGRIBALYSE (Koch and Salou 2016). These databases include most mineral (synthetic) fertilisers used in agriculture, but seldom and few organic amendments and fertilisers, with the occasional exception of raw or stored livestock manure, such as in the French database AGRIBALYSE. AGRIBALYSE is an agricultural life cycle inventories database, under continuous development (Koch and Salou 2014, 2015, 2016), featuring the main agricultural and livestock products and processes relevant to the French context, including certain products of tropical origin (Colomb et al. 2015).

In France, the agricultural recycling of organic waste is a common practice. For instance, in the 2011-2013 period, nearly 30% of the French agricultural surface, mainly field crops and prairies, received organic amendments and

fertilisers (Houot et al. 2014). In this study, the term "organic fertiliser" encompasses both residual organic amendments and fertilisers (ROAF)¹ and commercial organic amendments and fertilisers (COAF). ROAF are soil amending or fertilising products based on residual materials, often obtained from or- ganic waste treatments. They consist of organic residues either raw or transformed by relatively simple techniques: digestates, composts, concentrated agro-industrial effluents. COAF are products obtained from industrial processes, by means of for- mulations that include non-residual raw materials, whose main goal is precisely the manufacture of said products. They are often granulated for precise spreading (ANPEA 2017), and in France, approved with marketing authorisation granted by the National Agency for Food, Environmental and Occupational Health and Safety (ANSES), or standardised under by the French Standards Association (AFNOR). A de- tailed list of COAF commonly produced and used in France is presented in Constant (2011).

Not all products with fertilising value can be commercialised as fertilisers, as they do not meet (evolving) regulatory standards. Detailed expositions of the regulatory aspects of residues and organic amendments and fertilisers in France are presented in Houot et al. (2014) and Pradel (2017). ROAF/COAF are likely to receive one among three legal qualifications, namely "waste", "by-product", or "prod- uct", but the majority of ROAF/COAF currently in use have the status of "by-product" of an activity (e.g. livestock ma- nure) or "waste" (e.g. urban and industrial sewage sludge, certain composts). Materials considered as "waste" must be treated before disposal. When certain standardised criteria are met by ROAF/COAF (e.g. minimum/maximum content of nutrients, maximum contents of metal contaminants), they are considered as "products". These "products" include "amendments" and "fertilisers" proper, following the legally binding criteria applicable in France (see the Data in Brief article accompanying this study (Avadí et al. 2020)).

Approximately 120 Mt of ROAF, > 90% of which consisted of raw manures and slurries, were spread annually in France during the period 2011-2013 (Houot et al. 2014), versus between 8.6 and 9.1 Mt of synthetic fertilisers (http:// www.unifa.fr). The most recent figures of fertiliser deliveries in France are presented in Table 1. Organic fertilisers represented 28 to 34% of total fertiliser sales between 2010 and 2016 (ANPEA 2017); thus, the environmental perfor- mance of organic fertiliser production merits scholarship. The most common waste treatments yielding ROAF are composting (main substrates: green waste and sewage sludge) and anaerobic digestion (main substrates: liquid and solid ma- nures) (Houot et al. 2014). A variety of treatments are used,
1 Referred to among French institutions and researchers as "produits résiduaires organiques" (PRO) or "matières fertilisantes d'origine résiduaire" (MAFOR).
Table 1 Fertilisers delivered in France in 2017

Fertiliser type|Amount of product|Organic matter|N|P2O5|K2O Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised Mineral amendments (basic)|2.194|0|0.003|0.003|0.003 Mineral fertilisers|8.861|0|2.235|0.390|0.405 Commercial organo-mineral fertilisers|0.114|0.048|0.007|0.004|0.009 Commercial organic fertilisers|0.123|0.065|0.004|0.004|0.005 Commercial organic amendments|0.260|0.106|0.004|0.005|0.002 Residual organic fertilisers|1.826|0.725|0.045|0.042|0.057 Residual organic amendments|4.211|1.078|0.037|0.038|0.018 Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised Manure excreted while grazing (50% recovered as farmyard manure)||36.525|1.299|0.656|1.804 Other manures (locally spread)||19.279|0.720|0.382|0.970 Sewage sludge|0.660|0.495|0.038|0.024|0.008

Source: ANPEA (2018). All amounts are expressed in Mt (1 million t) fresh mass, except for sewage sludge, which is expressed in Mt DM. No recent statistics were available for sewage sludge. According to ADEME (2016), 1.1 Mt DM of sludge were produced in 2010, 60% of which were spread. Total organic matter, N, P, and K were calculated on the base of the characteristics of thickened sludge (Benoît et al. 2014)

usually specific to waste types, but very often combining di- verse substrates to obtain a final product with fertilising (i.e. N, P, K content) or amendment value (improvement of soil properties, such as C content). Ultimately, close to 100% of livestock effluents, 65% of organic agro-industrial residues, 54% of industrial organic residues (e.g. paper mill sludge), 75% of sewage sludge, and 14% of urban organic residues are recycled in French agricultural soils (Dhaouadi 2014; Houot et al. 2014). Of the ~ 120 Mt of ROAF spread annually in France in 2011-2013, 109 Mt consisted of raw manures, 4. 2 Mt of composted manures, 1.1 Mt of digestate from ma- nures, 2.1 Mt of treated agro-industrial residues, 0.9 Mt of treated sewage sludge, and 2.2 Mt of composts from urban and industrial residues (Houot et al. 2014). In a recent report, the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME) indicated that 729 Mt of organic amendments and fertilisers (94% of which consist of livestock effluents) are currently spread per year, 73% of sewage sludge is applied (31% after composting), 14.5% of organic waste is spread (after composting), 35% of organic industrial waste is spread (8% after composting), and 6.6 million ha of field crops and grasslands receive organic amendments and fertilisers (ADEME 2018). The goal of the study was to construct LCIs of the organic waste treatments yielding the most relevant organic amend- ments and fertilisers in France, which are also of European interest, as technologies and approaches-as well as the eco- nomic and political environment enabling them-do not vary much. It thus presents LCIs, constructed from mainly second- ary data-hence "screening"-of (a) the most relevant organ- ic waste treatments yielding ROAF and (b) industrial fertiliser production using certain organic residues as inputs and

yielding COAF, in France. It takes place under the ACV- MAFOR project, whose main goal is to provide LCIs of ROAF and COAF used in France to the AGRIBALYSE da- tabase. A screening LCA was performed on the newly pro- duced LCIs, to assess their relative impacts and to contrast them with reference values in the literature.

7: <header>1 Introduction </header>

Population growth, in combination with increasing urbanisa- tion, is generating large amounts of wastes (i.e. unwanted or unusable material, substances, or by-products), including or- ganic residues (i.e. fermentable/putrescible materials), whose management often represents a non-negligible cost factor. Simultaneously, agriculture demands increasing volumes of fertilising inputs, in a context of a rising global interest in more sustainable food production (Foster et al. 2006; Muller et al. 2017), but also of a rising food security challenge in many developing countries (Ingram et al. 2010; Godfray et al.

2012). In line with the increasingly popular concepts of Circular Economy and Green Economy (EC 2014; Loiseau et al. 2016), organic residues such as green waste, biowaste, wastewater treatment sludge, manures, and agricultural and agro-industrial residues are increasingly being recycled in ag- ricultural soils, often treated for hygienisation or concentra- tion, and/or otherwise rendered into organic-sourced fertiliser products (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Brockmann et al. 2018).

For environmental assessment, the ISO-standardised (ISO 2006) life cycle assessment (LCA) framework is widely used, due to its comprehensiveness (multiple impact categories) and flexibility, which allow the type of modelling adapted to rep- resent the key material and substance flows required for mean- ingful comparisons. Various methodological challenges re- main in agricultural LCA, especially when the goal includes system comparisons. One key challenge is the selection of a baseline system for comparison, given the fact that legislation forces the treatment of certain organic wastes before disposal, and that if waste disposal is included in the system, the func- tion delivered by the said system is expanded into fertilisation + waste disposal. Other standing challenges include the selec- tion of the right functional unit (Ponsioen and van der Werf 2015; Notarnicola et al. 2017), the allocation of impacts among co-products-including manure and crop residues- (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner 2015; Chen et al. 2016; Mackenzie et al. 2016), the consideration of contaminants transferred to soil (Bernstad and la Cour 2012; Laurent et al. 2014; Harder et al. 2015; Zang et al. 2015), the modelling of direct field emissions (Bockstaller et al. 2008, 2015), the com- putation of mineral fertiliser equivalents of organic fertilisers (Brockmann et al. 2018; Stedje et al. 2018), the consideration of soil quality changes (Benoist and Bessou 2018), and the availability of background life cycle data for modelling fertilisation. To deal with the latter, various national and inter- national agricultural life cycle inventory (LCI) databases have and are being built, such as ecoinvent (Nemecek and Kagi 2007; Nemecek and Schnetzer 2012), World Food LCA Database (Nemecek et al. 2014), Agri-footprint (Blonk Agri- footprint 2014) and AGRIBALYSE (Koch and Salou 2016). These databases include most mineral (synthetic) fertilisers used in agriculture, but seldom and few organic amendments and fertilisers, with the occasional exception of raw or stored livestock manure, such as in the French database AGRIBALYSE. AGRIBALYSE is an agricultural life cycle inventories database, under continuous development (Koch and Salou 2014, 2015, 2016), featuring the main agricultural and livestock products and processes relevant to the French context, including certain products of tropical origin (Colomb et al. 2015).

In France, the agricultural recycling of organic waste is a common practice. For instance, in the 2011-2013 period, nearly 30% of the French agricultural surface, mainly field crops and prairies, received organic amendments and

fertilisers (Houot et al. 2014). In this study, the term "organic fertiliser" encompasses both residual organic amendments and fertilisers (ROAF)¹ and commercial organic amendments and fertilisers (COAF). ROAF are soil amending or fertilising products based on residual materials, often obtained from or- ganic waste treatments. They consist of organic residues either raw or transformed by relatively simple techniques: digestates, composts, concentrated agro-industrial effluents. COAF are products obtained from industrial processes, by means of for- mulations that include non-residual raw materials, whose main goal is precisely the manufacture of said products. They are often granulated for precise spreading (ANPEA 2017), and in France, approved with marketing authorisation granted by the National Agency for Food, Environmental and Occupational Health and Safety (ANSES), or standardised under by the French Standards Association (AFNOR). A de- tailed list of COAF commonly produced and used in France is presented in Constant (2011).

Not all products with fertilising value can be commercialised as fertilisers, as they do not meet (evolving) regulatory standards. Detailed expositions of the regulatory aspects of residues and organic amendments and fertilisers in France are presented in Houot et al. (2014) and Pradel (2017). ROAF/COAF are likely to receive one among three legal qualifications, namely "waste", "by-product", or "prod- uct", but the majority of ROAF/COAF currently in use have the status of "by-product" of an activity (e.g. livestock ma- nure) or "waste" (e.g. urban and industrial sewage sludge, certain composts). Materials considered as "waste" must be treated before disposal. When certain standardised criteria are met by ROAF/COAF (e.g. minimum/maximum content of nutrients, maximum contents of metal contaminants), they are considered as "products". These "products" include "amendments" and "fertilisers" proper, following the legally binding criteria applicable in France (see the Data in Brief article accompanying this study (Avadí et al. 2020)).

Approximately 120 Mt of ROAF, > 90% of which consisted of raw manures and slurries, were spread annually in France during the period 2011-2013 (Houot et al. 2014), versus between 8.6 and 9.1 Mt of synthetic fertilisers (http:// www.unifa.fr). The most recent figures of fertiliser deliveries in France are presented in Table 1. Organic fertilisers represented 28 to 34% of total fertiliser sales between 2010 and 2016 (ANPEA 2017); thus, the environmental perfor- mance of organic fertiliser production merits scholarship. The most common waste treatments yielding ROAF are composting (main substrates: green waste and sewage sludge) and anaerobic digestion (main substrates: liquid and solid ma- nures) (Houot et al. 2014). A variety of treatments are used,
1 Referred to among French institutions and researchers as "produits résiduaires organiques" (PRO) or "matières fertilisantes d'origine résiduaire" (MAFOR).
Table 1 Fertilisers delivered in France in 2017

Fertiliser type|Amount of product|Organic matter|N|P2O5|K2O Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised Mineral amendments (basic)|2.194|0|0.003|0.003|0.003 Mineral fertilisers|8.861|0|2.235|0.390|0.405 Commercial organo-mineral fertilisers|0.114|0.048|0.007|0.004|0.009 Commercial organic fertilisers|0.123|0.065|0.004|0.004|0.005 Commercial organic amendments|0.260|0.106|0.004|0.005|0.002 Residual organic fertilisers|1.826|0.725|0.045|0.042|0.057 Residual organic amendments|4.211|1.078|0.037|0.038|0.018 Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised Manure excreted while grazing (50% recovered as farmyard manure)||36.525|1.299|0.656|1.804 Other manures (locally spread)||19.279|0.720|0.382|0.970 Sewage sludge|0.660|0.495|0.038|0.024|0.008

Source: ANPEA (2018). All amounts are expressed in Mt (1 million t) fresh mass, except for sewage sludge, which is expressed in Mt DM. No recent statistics were available for sewage sludge. According to ADEME (2016), 1.1 Mt DM of sludge were produced in 2010, 60% of which were spread. Total organic matter, N, P, and K were calculated on the base of the characteristics of thickened sludge (Benoît et al. 2014)

usually specific to waste types, but very often combining di- verse substrates to obtain a final product with fertilising (i.e. N, P, K content) or amendment value (improvement of soil properties, such as C content). Ultimately, close to 100% of livestock effluents, 65% of organic agro-industrial residues, 54% of industrial organic residues (e.g. paper mill sludge), 75% of sewage sludge, and 14% of urban organic residues are recycled in French agricultural soils (Dhaouadi 2014; Houot et al. 2014). Of the ~ 120 Mt of ROAF spread annually in France in 2011-2013, 109 Mt consisted of raw manures, 4. 2 Mt of composted manures, 1.1 Mt of digestate from ma- nures, 2.1 Mt of treated agro-industrial residues, 0.9 Mt of treated sewage sludge, and 2.2 Mt of composts from urban and industrial residues (Houot et al. 2014). In a recent report, the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME) indicated that 729 Mt of organic amendments and fertilisers (94% of which consist of livestock effluents) are currently spread per year, 73% of sewage sludge is applied (31% after composting), 14.5% of organic waste is spread (after composting), 35% of organic industrial waste is spread (8% after composting), and 6.6 million ha of field crops and grasslands receive organic amendments and fertilisers (ADEME 2018). The goal of the study was to construct LCIs of the organic waste treatments yielding the most relevant organic amend- ments and fertilisers in France, which are also of European interest, as technologies and approaches-as well as the eco- nomic and political environment enabling them-do not vary much. It thus presents LCIs, constructed from mainly second- ary data-hence "screening"-of (a) the most relevant organ- ic waste treatments yielding ROAF and (b) industrial fertiliser production using certain organic residues as inputs and

yielding COAF, in France. It takes place under the ACV- MAFOR project, whose main goal is to provide LCIs of ROAF and COAF used in France to the AGRIBALYSE da- tabase. A screening LCA was performed on the newly pro- duced LCIs, to assess their relative impacts and to contrast them with reference values in the literature.

Other standing challenges include the selec- tion of the right functional unit (Ponsioen and van der Werf 2015; Notarnicola et al. 2017), the allocation of impacts among co-products-including manure and crop residues- (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner 2015; Chen et al. 2016; Mackenzie et al. 2016), the consideration of contaminants transferred to soil (Bernstad and la Cour 2012; Laurent et al. 2014; Harder et al. 2015; Zang et al. 2015), the modelling of direct field emissions (Bockstaller et al. 2008, 2015), the com- putation of mineral fertiliser equivalents of organic fertilisers (Brockmann et al. 2018; Stedje et al. 2018), the consideration of soil quality changes (Benoist and Bessou 2018), and the availability of background life cycle data for modelling fertilisation. To deal with the latter, various national and inter- national agricultural life cycle inventory (LCI) databases have and are being built, such as ecoinvent (Nemecek and Kagi 2007; Nemecek and Schnetzer 2012), World Food LCA Database (Nemecek et al. 2014), Agri-footprint (Blonk Agri- footprint 2014) and AGRIBALYSE (Koch and Salou 2016). These databases include most mineral (synthetic) fertilisers used in agriculture, but seldom and few organic amendments and fertilisers, with the occasional exception of raw or stored livestock manure, such as in the French database AGRIBALYSE. AGRIBALYSE is an agricultural life cycle inventories database, under continuous development (Koch and Salou 2014, 2015, 2016), featuring the main agricultural and livestock products and processes relevant to the French context, including certain products of tropical origin (Colomb et al. 2015).

In France, the agricultural recycling of organic waste is a common practice. For instance, in the 2011-2013 period, nearly 30% of the French agricultural surface, mainly field crops and prairies, received organic amendments and

fertilisers (Houot et al. 2014). In this study, the term "organic fertiliser" encompasses both residual organic amendments and fertilisers (ROAF)¹ and commercial organic amendments and fertilisers (COAF). ROAF are soil amending or fertilising products based on residual materials, often obtained from or- ganic waste treatments. They consist of organic residues either raw or transformed by relatively simple techniques: digestates, composts, concentrated agro-industrial effluents. COAF are products obtained from industrial processes, by means of for- mulations that include non-residual raw materials, whose main goal is precisely the manufacture of said products. They are often granulated for precise spreading (ANPEA 2017), and in France, approved with marketing authorisation granted by the National Agency for Food, Environmental and Occupational Health and Safety (ANSES), or standardised under by the French Standards Association (AFNOR). A de- tailed list of COAF commonly produced and used in France is presented in Constant (2011).

Not all products with fertilising value can be commercialised as fertilisers, as they do not meet (evolving) regulatory standards. Detailed expositions of the regulatory aspects of residues and organic amendments and fertilisers in France are presented in Houot et al. (2014) and Pradel (2017). ROAF/COAF are likely to receive one among three legal qualifications, namely "waste", "by-product", or "prod- uct", but the majority of ROAF/COAF currently in use have the status of "by-product" of an activity (e.g. livestock ma- nure) or "waste" (e.g. urban and industrial sewage sludge, certain composts). Materials considered as "waste" must be treated before disposal. When certain standardised criteria are met by ROAF/COAF (e.g. minimum/maximum content of nutrients, maximum contents of metal contaminants), they are considered as "products". These "products" include "amendments" and "fertilisers" proper, following the legally binding criteria applicable in France (see the Data in Brief article accompanying this study (Avadí et al. 2020)).

Approximately 120 Mt of ROAF, > 90% of which consisted of raw manures and slurries, were spread annually in France during the period 2011-2013 (Houot et al. 2014), versus between 8.6 and 9.1 Mt of synthetic fertilisers (http:// www.unifa.fr). The most recent figures of fertiliser deliveries in France are presented in Table 1. Organic fertilisers represented 28 to 34% of total fertiliser sales between 2010 and 2016 (ANPEA 2017); thus, the environmental perfor- mance of organic fertiliser production merits scholarship. The most common waste treatments yielding ROAF are composting (main substrates: green waste and sewage sludge) and anaerobic digestion (main substrates: liquid and solid ma- nures) (Houot et al. 2014). A variety of treatments are used,
1 Referred to among French institutions and researchers as "produits résiduaires organiques" (PRO) or "matières fertilisantes d'origine résiduaire" (MAFOR).
Table 1 Fertilisers delivered in France in 2017

Fertiliser type|Amount of product|Organic matter|N|P2O5|K2O Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised|Commercialised Mineral amendments (basic)|2.194|0|0.003|0.003|0.003 Mineral fertilisers|8.861|0|2.235|0.390|0.405 Commercial organo-mineral fertilisers|0.114|0.048|0.007|0.004|0.009 Commercial organic fertilisers|0.123|0.065|0.004|0.004|0.005 Commercial organic amendments|0.260|0.106|0.004|0.005|0.002 Residual organic fertilisers|1.826|0.725|0.045|0.042|0.057 Residual organic amendments|4.211|1.078|0.037|0.038|0.018 Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised|Non-commercialised Manure excreted while grazing (50% recovered as farmyard manure)||36.525|1.299|0.656|1.804 Other manures (locally spread)||19.279|0.720|0.382|0.970 Sewage sludge|0.660|0.495|0.038|0.024|0.008

Source: ANPEA (2018). All amounts are expressed in Mt (1 million t) fresh mass, except for sewage sludge, which is expressed in Mt DM. No recent statistics were available for sewage sludge. According to ADEME (2016), 1.1 Mt DM of sludge were produced in 2010, 60% of which were spread. Total organic matter, N, P, and K were calculated on the base of the characteristics of thickened sludge (Benoît et al. 2014)

usually specific to waste types, but very often combining di- verse substrates to obtain a final product with fertilising (i.e. N, P, K content) or amendment value (improvement of soil properties, such as C content). Ultimately, close to 100% of livestock effluents, 65% of organic agro-industrial residues, 54% of industrial organic residues (e.g. paper mill sludge), 75% of sewage sludge, and 14% of urban organic residues are recycled in French agricultural soils (Dhaouadi 2014; Houot et al. 2014). Of the ~ 120 Mt of ROAF spread annually in France in 2011-2013, 109 Mt consisted of raw manures, 4. 2 Mt of composted manures, 1.1 Mt of digestate from ma- nures, 2.1 Mt of treated agro-industrial residues, 0.9 Mt of treated sewage sludge, and 2.2 Mt of composts from urban and industrial residues (Houot et al. 2014). In a recent report, the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME) indicated that 729 Mt of organic amendments and fertilisers (94% of which consist of livestock effluents) are currently spread per year, 73% of sewage sludge is applied (31% after composting), 14.5% of organic waste is spread (after composting), 35% of organic industrial waste is spread (8% after composting), and 6.6 million ha of field crops and grasslands receive organic amendments and fertilisers (ADEME 2018). The goal of the study was to construct LCIs of the organic waste treatments yielding the most relevant organic amend- ments and fertilisers in France, which are also of European interest, as technologies and approaches-as well as the eco- nomic and political environment enabling them-do not vary much. It thus presents LCIs, constructed from mainly second- ary data-hence "screening"-of (a) the most relevant organ- ic waste treatments yielding ROAF and (b) industrial fertiliser production using certain organic residues as inputs and

yielding COAF, in France. It takes place under the ACV- MAFOR project, whose main goal is to provide LCIs of ROAF and COAF used in France to the AGRIBALYSE da- tabase. A screening LCA was performed on the newly pro- duced LCIs, to assess their relative impacts and to contrast them with reference values in the literature.

To deal with the latter, various national and inter- national agricultural life cycle inventory (LCI) databases have and are being built, such as ecoinvent (Nemecek and Kagi 2007; Nemecek and Schnetzer 2012), World Food LCA Database (Nemecek et al. 2014), Agri-footprint (Blonk Agri- footprint 2014) and AGRIBALYSE (Koch and Salou 2016). These databases include most mineral (synthetic) fertilisers used in agriculture, but seldom and few organic amendments and fertilisers, with the occasional exception of raw or stored livestock manure, such as in the French database AGRIBALYSE. AGRIBALYSE is an agricultural life cycle inventories database, under continuous development (Koch and Salou 2014, 2015, 2016), featuring the main agricultural and livestock products and processes relevant to the French context, including certain products of tropical origin (Colomb et al. 2015).

In France, the agricultural recycling of organic waste is a common practice. For instance, in the 2011-2013 period, nearly 30% of the French agricultural surface, mainly field crops and prairies, received organic amendments and

fertilisers (Houot et al. 2014). In this study, the term "organic fertiliser" encompasses both residual organic amendments and fertilisers (ROAF)¹ and commercial organic amendments and fertilisers (COAF). ROAF are soil amending or fertilising products based on residual materials, often obtained from or- ganic waste treatments. They consist of organic residues either raw or transformed by relatively simple techniques: digestates, composts, concentrated agro-industrial effluents. COAF are products obtained from industrial processes, by means of for- mulations that include non-residual raw materials, whose main goal is precisely the manufacture of said products. They are often granulated for precise spreading (ANPEA 2017), and in France, approved with marketing authorisation granted by the National Agency for Food, Environmental and Occupational Health and Safety (ANSES), or standardised under by the French Standards Association (AFNOR). A de- tailed list of COAF commonly produced and used in France is presented in Constant (2011).

Not all products with fertilising value can be commercialised as fertilisers, as they do not meet (evolving) regulatory standards. Detailed expositions of the regulatory aspects of residues and organic amendments and fertilisers in France are presented in Houot et al. (2014) and Pradel (2017). ROAF/COAF are likely to receive one among three legal qualifications, namely "waste", "by-product", or "prod- uct", but the majority of ROAF/COAF currently in use have the status of "by-product" of an activity (e.g. livestock ma- nure) or "waste" (e.g. urban and industrial sewage sludge, certain composts). Materials considered as "waste" must be treated before disposal. When certain standardised criteria are met by ROAF/COAF (e.g. minimum/maximum content of nutrients, maximum contents of metal contaminants), they are considered as "products". These "products" include "amendments" and "fertilisers" proper, following the legally binding criteria applicable in France (see the Data in Brief article accompanying this study (Avadí et al. 2020)).

Approximately 120 Mt of ROAF, > 90% of which consisted of raw manures and slurries, were spread annually in France during the period 2011-2013 (Houot et al. 2014), versus between 8.6 and 9.1 Mt of synthetic fertilisers (http:// www.unifa.fr). The most recent figures of fertiliser deliveries in France are presented in Table 1. Organic fertilisers represented 28 to 34% of total fertiliser sales between 2010 and 2016 (ANPEA 2017); thus, the environmental perfor- mance of organic fertiliser production merits scholarship. The most common waste treatments yielding ROAF are composting (main substrates: green waste and sewage sludge) and anaerobic digestion (main substrates: liquid and solid ma- nures) (Houot et al. 2014). A variety of treatments are used,

usually specific to waste types, but very often combining di- verse substrates to obtain a final product with fertilising (i.e. N, P, K content) or amendment value (improvement of soil properties, such as C content). Ultimately, close to 100% of livestock effluents, 65% of organic agro-industrial residues, 54% of industrial organic residues (e.g. paper mill sludge), 75% of sewage sludge, and 14% of urban organic residues are recycled in French agricultural soils (Dhaouadi 2014; Houot et al. 2014). Of the ~ 120 Mt of ROAF spread annually in France in 2011-2013, 109 Mt consisted of raw manures, 4. 2 Mt of composted manures, 1.1 Mt of digestate from ma- nures, 2.1 Mt of treated agro-industrial residues, 0.9 Mt of treated sewage sludge, and 2.2 Mt of composts from urban and industrial residues (Houot et al. 2014). In a recent report, the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME) indicated that 729 Mt of organic amendments and fertilisers (94% of which consist of livestock effluents) are currently spread per year, 73% of sewage sludge is applied (31% after composting), 14.5% of organic waste is spread (after composting), 35% of organic industrial waste is spread (8% after composting), and 6.6 million ha of field crops and grasslands receive organic amendments and fertilisers (ADEME 2018). The goal of the study was to construct LCIs of the organic waste treatments yielding the most relevant organic amend- ments and fertilisers in France, which are also of European interest, as technologies and approaches-as well as the eco- nomic and political environment enabling them-do not vary much. It thus presents LCIs, constructed from mainly second- ary data-hence "screening"-of (a) the most relevant organ- ic waste treatments yielding ROAF and (b) industrial fertiliser production using certain organic residues as inputs and

yielding COAF, in France. It takes place under the ACV- MAFOR project, whose main goal is to provide LCIs of ROAF and COAF used in France to the AGRIBALYSE da- tabase. A screening LCA was performed on the newly pro- duced LCIs, to assess their relative impacts and to contrast them with reference values in the literature.
14: <header>2.1 Goal and scope </header>

The main goal of the study is to compare the impacts of pro- ducing fertilisers from organic residues, excluding their use as amendments or fertilisers (see Section 2.2.1), in a French and European context. Therefore, the discussion of direct field emissions-associated with a crop's N-balance (Bockstaller and Girardin 2010; COMIFER 2013)-delayed release of nu- trients in plant-available form, and the long-term effects of repeated ROAF/COAF application on soil quality exceed the scope of this work, and will be addressed in future publi- cations (under preparation). This study aims at informing both AGRIBALYSE users and future modelling on the rationale behind organic amendments and fertilisers LCIs developed for AGRIBALYSE.

The life cycle of organic residues recycled in agricultural soils encompasses the generation, treatment and spreading of said residues (Fig. 1, System A). Sometimes, the generation and treatment of organic residues take place in the same sys- tem, such as the case of farms treating their animal effluents and harvest residues to be used in the same farm or in others (Fig. 1, System B). Nonetheless, very often the generation,

organic residues

Generation co-products of organic Storage|Generation co-products of organic Storage||||Transport System C1: Production of organic residues (including storage)|treatment Transport Pre- Intermediate products Treatment Intermediate and Reception storage Transport storage step n n|treatment Transport Pre- Intermediate products Treatment Intermediate and Reception storage Transport storage step n n||Transport Storage|Transport System B: generation and (partial or full) treatment|System production chemicals) (transportation, C2: Treatment of organic storage, amendments of organic direct emissions, residues and fertilisers and fuels,|System production chemicals) (transportation, C2: Treatment of organic storage, amendments of organic direct emissions, residues and fertilisers and fuels,|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct
System A: generation, treatment and use

treatment and use of organic residues take place under differ- ent systems at different locations (Fig. 1, System C). In this study, the scope of the inventories includes the pro- vision of substrates (i.e. organic residues without commercial value and featuring no or few impacts, and organic co- products with commercial value featuring impacts from a co-production), transportation and storage (before, during and after processing), and treatments (Fig. 1, System C2). It is a gate-to-gate scope, which excludes the production and initial storage of organic residues and co-products (Fig. 1, System C1), as well as transportation to the receiving farm, storage and spreading (Fig. 1, System C3).

Moreover, following the "action-flow-stock ontology" (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Soulié and Wassenaar 2017), it is envisaged that the system models proposed in this work would be used as building blocks for agricultural LCAs where the treatment of organic residues may constitute a separate system than those generating and using them. The proposed approach would thus allow LCA modelling of different types of sys- items: storage immediate to the generation of residues (Fig. 1, System C1); treatment of residues (Fig. 1, System C2); storage immediate to the generation and (partial or full) treatment of residues (Fig. 1, System B); storage previous to the use of organic amendments and fertilisers (Fig. 1, System C3); and integrated systems including storage immediate to the gener- ation, treatment, transportation and storage previous to use (Fig. 1, System A). The generation of raw organic residues and use of treatment products is completely beyond the scope of this work, and would require additional LCI models/ building blocks.

Two design elements were particularly troublesome, name- ly, the choice of a functional unit or units enabling fair com- parisons, and the allocation of treatment co-products (espe- cially of treatments such as anaerobic digestion, which yield economically interesting co-products).

The LCIs were constructed using 1 t fresh mass of final product, complemented with 1 t of N, P, and K in fresh mass as the main functional units, but additional functional units were retained for comparisons, as detailed in Section 2.3: 1 t of each nutrient in forms readily available to the plants and 1 t of C, both in product fresh mass. The rationale behind all these functional units is that the provision of nutrients to crops is assumed to be the main driver for the production of both organic and mineral fertilisers. There are of course cases, such as in viticulture, where the main driver for the use of ROAF/ COAF may be to improve soil properties (i.e. amendment rather than fertilisation). All flows are represented in terms of fresh mass, instead of dry matter, because final users of ROAF/COAF apply them to soils after estimating their nutri- ents or organic matter contents in terms of fresh mass. Composition data was moreover compiled for all modelled ROAF and COAF (Avadí et al. 2020), and determined that the water content of these products is highly variable (i.e. it is distributed along a wide range), and thus expressing invento- ries in terms of dry matter would disregard variability and, ultimately, uncertainty.

Organic fertilisation adds organic carbon to soils, and it has been observed that organically-fertilised soil tends to feature higher soil organic matter (Tuomisto et al. 2012; Vicente- Vicente et al. 2016). Such increase in soil organic matter (i.e.

C) associated with organic fertilisation has been demonstrated, for instance, by the French long-term field experiment QualiAgro (Peltre et al. 2012; Obriot et al. 2016). Nonetheless, the capability of long-term organic fertilisation to sequester more organic C in soils than conventional (i.e. mineral or organo-mineral) fertilisation has been called into question by Kirchmann et al. (2016), with the argument that the main contributor to soil organic C is crop biomass, and that systems applying organic fertilisation only (e.g. organic agri- culture) feature lower yields than conventional ones. In con- sequence, as there is no consensus that organic fertilisation leads to increased soil C, and no agreed-upon method ac- counts for all C fluxes and stocks in soils (Benoist and Bessou 2018), total C content was retained only as an auxil- iary functional unit enabling certain comparisons (see Section 2.3).
Regarding allocation of impacts among co-products, the following strategies were followed:

<list>a. All untreated residues of agricultural origin were as- sumed to carry no environmental burden, following the AGRIBALYSE methodology and in line with the dominant practice in French LCA community. For in- stance, livestock effluents are not considered as co- products but as by-products with low value, SO no im- pact of the livestock system was allocated to the efflu- ents, but only the downstream impacts of processing and transport. Similarly, untreated sewage sludge was also assumed to have zero impact, as it is a by-product (considered as waste) from wastewater treatment; thus, only the impacts of further sludge treatment were con- sidered. Pressed cakes and husks from oily seed pro- cessing, as well as other agro-industrial residues used in COAF production, on the other hand, were instead allocated (by a mass-weighted economic key using lit- erature data) a portion of the energy and water require- ments of processing, but no impacts of their upstream agricultural phase. 
b. When waste treatments (other than phase separation) pro- duced water losses such as evaporation and lixiviation, these losses were handled as emissions, and all impacts were assigned to the resulting ROAF. 
C. In sludge treatments, thickening and dewatering of sludge were assumed to be part of the ROAF production and not of the wastewater treatment, following Pradel (2016), de- spite being a standard process in wastewater treatment plants, aimed at sludge disposal. Moreover, when a spe- cific technique is not explicitly indicated, it can be as- sumed that the LCI model of any ROAF whose treatment pathway includes thickening will include an average of thickening technologies, weighted by the amount of pop- ulation equivalents treated by each one (Richard and Pradel 2014).</list>

<list>d. When treatments such as anaerobic digestion produced also energy carriers (i.e. biogas), impacts were allocated following the rules of the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME), which recom- mends economic allocation in cases when the co- products have a different purpose or characteristic of in- terest, such as their energy content and their agronomic value. Economic allocation keys from Rehl et al. (2012) were thus retained. 
e. Background processes were extracted from ecoinvent (un- allocated or economically allocated) and AGRIBALYSE, including agricultural substrates. These processes include infrastructure (e.g. storage), transport (e.g. waste collec- tion), energy (electricity, fuels) and agricultural residues (e.g. straw). All N fertiliser inputs are allocated among all crops in a rotation for AGRIBALYSE crops. 
f. A final allocation-like consideration was the modelling of water separated during sewage sludge treatments. These water emissions were modelled as "wastewater to river", because if modelled as "water to river", a bonus (i.e. a negative emission) would be estimated and the impacts of these treatments consequently reduced. These separated waters feature a non-negligible solid matter load, and thus can be considered as wastewater.</list>

The ILCD-recommended collection of life cycle impact assessment methods (EC-JRC 2012), ILCD 2011 Midpoint+ v1.10, as implemented in SimaPro v.8.5.2 (which includes a single score but no aggregation into areas of protection), was used to estimate the production impacts of all modelled ROAF, in order to enable comparisons among them and against previously published results, of both organic and syn- thetic fertilisers. The method includes 16 midpoint impact categories: climate change (IPCC 2007), ozone depletion, hu- man toxicity-cancer effects (USEtox), human toxicity-non- cancer effects (USEtox), particulate matter, ionising radiation-human health, ionising radiation-ecosystems, photo- chemical ozone formation, acidification, terrestrial eutrophi- cation, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity (USEtox), land use (soil organic mat- ter), and mineral, fossil, and renewable resource depletion. It computes the single score applying equal weighting to all impact categories. The characterisation factor for climate change of "CO2 in air" was updated from - 1 to 0 (carbon- neutral instead of sequestration), because it was considered that CO2 in air absorbed by plants and re-released in the short term should not be treated as a sequestration. Single scores were retained because they allow harnessing the comparative power of LCA in the simplest, more accessible to non-expert fashion. Moreover, in this work, the comparative impact in- tensity is more relevant than absolute assessments.
AGRIBALYSE v1.3 and ecoinvent v3.4 were used as sources of background inventory data. Foreground data was

obtained mainly from scientific (articles) and grey literature (books, project reports, unpublished datasets), in only rough detail, and thus, the resulting life cycle impact assessment results are referred to as a "screening" LCA (Wenzel 1998). The dominant selection criteria were availability and geographical/technological pertinence. The only primary data collected were those pertaining to COAF, provided by anon- ymous producers, as well as a few instances of ROAF. The resulting inventories, as well as their comparative im- pacts, were compared with literature values and subject to vetting by French experts (i.e. the co-authors of the accompa- nying data paper Avadí et al. (2020)) on the environmental assessment of the different modelled treatment routes.

Generation co-products of organic Storage|Generation co-products of organic Storage||||Transport System C1: Production of organic residues (including storage)|treatment Transport Pre- Intermediate products Treatment Intermediate and Reception storage Transport storage step n n|treatment Transport Pre- Intermediate products Treatment Intermediate and Reception storage Transport storage step n n||Transport Storage|Transport System B: generation and (partial or full) treatment|System production chemicals) (transportation, C2: Treatment of organic storage, amendments of organic direct emissions, residues and fertilisers and fuels,|System production chemicals) (transportation, C2: Treatment of organic storage, amendments of organic direct emissions, residues and fertilisers and fuels,|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct
System A: generation, treatment and use

treatment and use of organic residues take place under differ- ent systems at different locations (Fig. 1, System C). In this study, the scope of the inventories includes the pro- vision of substrates (i.e. organic residues without commercial value and featuring no or few impacts, and organic co- products with commercial value featuring impacts from a co-production), transportation and storage (before, during and after processing), and treatments (Fig. 1, System C2). It is a gate-to-gate scope, which excludes the production and initial storage of organic residues and co-products (Fig. 1, System C1), as well as transportation to the receiving farm, storage and spreading (Fig. 1, System C3).

Moreover, following the "action-flow-stock ontology" (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Soulié and Wassenaar 2017), it is envisaged that the system models proposed in this work would be used as building blocks for agricultural LCAs where the treatment of organic residues may constitute a separate system than those generating and using them. The proposed approach would thus allow LCA modelling of different types of sys- items: storage immediate to the generation of residues (Fig. 1, System C1); treatment of residues (Fig. 1, System C2); storage immediate to the generation and (partial or full) treatment of residues (Fig. 1, System B); storage previous to the use of organic amendments and fertilisers (Fig. 1, System C3); and integrated systems including storage immediate to the gener- ation, treatment, transportation and storage previous to use (Fig. 1, System A). The generation of raw organic residues and use of treatment products is completely beyond the scope of this work, and would require additional LCI models/ building blocks.

Two design elements were particularly troublesome, name- ly, the choice of a functional unit or units enabling fair com- parisons, and the allocation of treatment co-products (espe- cially of treatments such as anaerobic digestion, which yield economically interesting co-products).

The LCIs were constructed using 1 t fresh mass of final product, complemented with 1 t of N, P, and K in fresh mass as the main functional units, but additional functional units were retained for comparisons, as detailed in Section 2.3: 1 t of each nutrient in forms readily available to the plants and 1 t of C, both in product fresh mass. The rationale behind all these functional units is that the provision of nutrients to crops is assumed to be the main driver for the production of both organic and mineral fertilisers. There are of course cases, such as in viticulture, where the main driver for the use of ROAF/ COAF may be to improve soil properties (i.e. amendment rather than fertilisation). All flows are represented in terms of fresh mass, instead of dry matter, because final users of ROAF/COAF apply them to soils after estimating their nutri- ents or organic matter contents in terms of fresh mass. Composition data was moreover compiled for all modelled ROAF and COAF (Avadí et al. 2020), and determined that the water content of these products is highly variable (i.e. it is distributed along a wide range), and thus expressing invento- ries in terms of dry matter would disregard variability and, ultimately, uncertainty.

Organic fertilisation adds organic carbon to soils, and it has been observed that organically-fertilised soil tends to feature higher soil organic matter (Tuomisto et al. 2012; Vicente- Vicente et al. 2016). Such increase in soil organic matter (i.e.

C) associated with organic fertilisation has been demonstrated, for instance, by the French long-term field experiment QualiAgro (Peltre et al. 2012; Obriot et al. 2016). Nonetheless, the capability of long-term organic fertilisation to sequester more organic C in soils than conventional (i.e. mineral or organo-mineral) fertilisation has been called into question by Kirchmann et al. (2016), with the argument that the main contributor to soil organic C is crop biomass, and that systems applying organic fertilisation only (e.g. organic agri- culture) feature lower yields than conventional ones. In con- sequence, as there is no consensus that organic fertilisation leads to increased soil C, and no agreed-upon method ac- counts for all C fluxes and stocks in soils (Benoist and Bessou 2018), total C content was retained only as an auxil- iary functional unit enabling certain comparisons (see Section 2.3).
Regarding allocation of impacts among co-products, the following strategies were followed:

<list>a. All untreated residues of agricultural origin were as- sumed to carry no environmental burden, following the AGRIBALYSE methodology and in line with the dominant practice in French LCA community. For in- stance, livestock effluents are not considered as co- products but as by-products with low value, SO no im- pact of the livestock system was allocated to the efflu- ents, but only the downstream impacts of processing and transport. Similarly, untreated sewage sludge was also assumed to have zero impact, as it is a by-product (considered as waste) from wastewater treatment; thus, only the impacts of further sludge treatment were con- sidered. Pressed cakes and husks from oily seed pro- cessing, as well as other agro-industrial residues used in COAF production, on the other hand, were instead allocated (by a mass-weighted economic key using lit- erature data) a portion of the energy and water require- ments of processing, but no impacts of their upstream agricultural phase. 
b. When waste treatments (other than phase separation) pro- duced water losses such as evaporation and lixiviation, these losses were handled as emissions, and all impacts were assigned to the resulting ROAF. 
C. In sludge treatments, thickening and dewatering of sludge were assumed to be part of the ROAF production and not of the wastewater treatment, following Pradel (2016), de- spite being a standard process in wastewater treatment plants, aimed at sludge disposal. Moreover, when a spe- cific technique is not explicitly indicated, it can be as- sumed that the LCI model of any ROAF whose treatment pathway includes thickening will include an average of thickening technologies, weighted by the amount of pop- ulation equivalents treated by each one (Richard and Pradel 2014).</list>

<list>d. When treatments such as anaerobic digestion produced also energy carriers (i.e. biogas), impacts were allocated following the rules of the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME), which recom- mends economic allocation in cases when the co- products have a different purpose or characteristic of in- terest, such as their energy content and their agronomic value. Economic allocation keys from Rehl et al. (2012) were thus retained. 
e. Background processes were extracted from ecoinvent (un- allocated or economically allocated) and AGRIBALYSE, including agricultural substrates. These processes include infrastructure (e.g. storage), transport (e.g. waste collec- tion), energy (electricity, fuels) and agricultural residues (e.g. straw). All N fertiliser inputs are allocated among all crops in a rotation for AGRIBALYSE crops. 
f. A final allocation-like consideration was the modelling of water separated during sewage sludge treatments. These water emissions were modelled as "wastewater to river", because if modelled as "water to river", a bonus (i.e. a negative emission) would be estimated and the impacts of these treatments consequently reduced. These separated waters feature a non-negligible solid matter load, and thus can be considered as wastewater.</list>

The ILCD-recommended collection of life cycle impact assessment methods (EC-JRC 2012), ILCD 2011 Midpoint+ v1.10, as implemented in SimaPro v.8.5.2 (which includes a single score but no aggregation into areas of protection), was used to estimate the production impacts of all modelled ROAF, in order to enable comparisons among them and against previously published results, of both organic and syn- thetic fertilisers. The method includes 16 midpoint impact categories: climate change (IPCC 2007), ozone depletion, hu- man toxicity-cancer effects (USEtox), human toxicity-non- cancer effects (USEtox), particulate matter, ionising radiation-human health, ionising radiation-ecosystems, photo- chemical ozone formation, acidification, terrestrial eutrophi- cation, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity (USEtox), land use (soil organic mat- ter), and mineral, fossil, and renewable resource depletion. It computes the single score applying equal weighting to all impact categories. The characterisation factor for climate change of "CO2 in air" was updated from - 1 to 0 (carbon- neutral instead of sequestration), because it was considered that CO2 in air absorbed by plants and re-released in the short term should not be treated as a sequestration. Single scores were retained because they allow harnessing the comparative power of LCA in the simplest, more accessible to non-expert fashion. Moreover, in this work, the comparative impact in- tensity is more relevant than absolute assessments.
AGRIBALYSE v1.3 and ecoinvent v3.4 were used as sources of background inventory data. Foreground data was

obtained mainly from scientific (articles) and grey literature (books, project reports, unpublished datasets), in only rough detail, and thus, the resulting life cycle impact assessment results are referred to as a "screening" LCA (Wenzel 1998). The dominant selection criteria were availability and geographical/technological pertinence. The only primary data collected were those pertaining to COAF, provided by anon- ymous producers, as well as a few instances of ROAF. The resulting inventories, as well as their comparative im- pacts, were compared with literature values and subject to vetting by French experts (i.e. the co-authors of the accompa- nying data paper Avadí et al. (2020)) on the environmental assessment of the different modelled treatment routes.
17: <header>2.1 Goal and scope </header>

The main goal of the study is to compare the impacts of pro- ducing fertilisers from organic residues, excluding their use as amendments or fertilisers (see Section 2.2.1), in a French and European context. Therefore, the discussion of direct field emissions-associated with a crop's N-balance (Bockstaller and Girardin 2010; COMIFER 2013)-delayed release of nu- trients in plant-available form, and the long-term effects of repeated ROAF/COAF application on soil quality exceed the scope of this work, and will be addressed in future publi- cations (under preparation). This study aims at informing both AGRIBALYSE users and future modelling on the rationale behind organic amendments and fertilisers LCIs developed for AGRIBALYSE.

The life cycle of organic residues recycled in agricultural soils encompasses the generation, treatment and spreading of said residues (Fig. 1, System A). Sometimes, the generation and treatment of organic residues take place in the same sys- tem, such as the case of farms treating their animal effluents and harvest residues to be used in the same farm or in others (Fig. 1, System B). Nonetheless, very often the generation,

19: <header>2.1 Goal and scope </header>

The main goal of the study is to compare the impacts of pro- ducing fertilisers from organic residues, excluding their use as amendments or fertilisers (see Section 2.2.1), in a French and European context. Therefore, the discussion of direct field emissions-associated with a crop's N-balance (Bockstaller and Girardin 2010; COMIFER 2013)-delayed release of nu- trients in plant-available form, and the long-term effects of repeated ROAF/COAF application on soil quality exceed the scope of this work, and will be addressed in future publi- cations (under preparation). This study aims at informing both AGRIBALYSE users and future modelling on the rationale behind organic amendments and fertilisers LCIs developed for AGRIBALYSE.

The life cycle of organic residues recycled in agricultural soils encompasses the generation, treatment and spreading of said residues (Fig. 1, System A). Sometimes, the generation and treatment of organic residues take place in the same sys- tem, such as the case of farms treating their animal effluents and harvest residues to be used in the same farm or in others (Fig. 1, System B). Nonetheless, very often the generation,

Generation of
Storage
organic residues

Generation co-products of organic Storage|Generation co-products of organic Storage||||Transport System C1: Production of organic residues (including storage)|treatment Transport Pre- Intermediate products Treatment Intermediate and Reception storage Transport storage step n n|treatment Transport Pre- Intermediate products Treatment Intermediate and Reception storage Transport storage step n n||Transport Storage|Transport System B: generation and (partial or full) treatment|System production chemicals) (transportation, C2: Treatment of organic storage, amendments of organic direct emissions, residues and fertilisers and fuels,|System production chemicals) (transportation, C2: Treatment of organic storage, amendments of organic direct emissions, residues and fertilisers and fuels,|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct
System A: generation, treatment and use

treatment and use of organic residues take place under differ- ent systems at different locations (Fig. 1, System C). In this study, the scope of the inventories includes the pro- vision of substrates (i.e. organic residues without commercial value and featuring no or few impacts, and organic co- products with commercial value featuring impacts from a co-production), transportation and storage (before, during and after processing), and treatments (Fig. 1, System C2). It is a gate-to-gate scope, which excludes the production and initial storage of organic residues and co-products (Fig. 1, System C1), as well as transportation to the receiving farm, storage and spreading (Fig. 1, System C3).

Moreover, following the "action-flow-stock ontology" (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Soulié and Wassenaar 2017), it is envisaged that the system models proposed in this work would be used as building blocks for agricultural LCAs where the treatment of organic residues may constitute a separate system than those generating and using them. The proposed approach would thus allow LCA modelling of different types of sys- items: storage immediate to the generation of residues (Fig. 1, System C1); treatment of residues (Fig. 1, System C2); storage immediate to the generation and (partial or full) treatment of residues (Fig. 1, System B); storage previous to the use of organic amendments and fertilisers (Fig. 1, System C3); and integrated systems including storage immediate to the gener- ation, treatment, transportation and storage previous to use (Fig. 1, System A). The generation of raw organic residues and use of treatment products is completely beyond the scope of this work, and would require additional LCI models/ building blocks.

Two design elements were particularly troublesome, name- ly, the choice of a functional unit or units enabling fair com- parisons, and the allocation of treatment co-products (espe- cially of treatments such as anaerobic digestion, which yield economically interesting co-products).

The LCIs were constructed using 1 t fresh mass of final product, complemented with 1 t of N, P, and K in fresh mass as the main functional units, but additional functional units were retained for comparisons, as detailed in Section 2.3: 1 t of each nutrient in forms readily available to the plants and 1 t of C, both in product fresh mass. The rationale behind all these functional units is that the provision of nutrients to crops is assumed to be the main driver for the production of both organic and mineral fertilisers. There are of course cases, such as in viticulture, where the main driver for the use of ROAF/ COAF may be to improve soil properties (i.e. amendment rather than fertilisation). All flows are represented in terms of fresh mass, instead of dry matter, because final users of ROAF/COAF apply them to soils after estimating their nutri- ents or organic matter contents in terms of fresh mass. Composition data was moreover compiled for all modelled ROAF and COAF (Avadí et al. 2020), and determined that the water content of these products is highly variable (i.e. it is distributed along a wide range), and thus expressing invento- ries in terms of dry matter would disregard variability and, ultimately, uncertainty.

Organic fertilisation adds organic carbon to soils, and it has been observed that organically-fertilised soil tends to feature higher soil organic matter (Tuomisto et al. 2012; Vicente- Vicente et al. 2016). Such increase in soil organic matter (i.e.

C) associated with organic fertilisation has been demonstrated, for instance, by the French long-term field experiment QualiAgro (Peltre et al. 2012; Obriot et al. 2016). Nonetheless, the capability of long-term organic fertilisation to sequester more organic C in soils than conventional (i.e. mineral or organo-mineral) fertilisation has been called into question by Kirchmann et al. (2016), with the argument that the main contributor to soil organic C is crop biomass, and that systems applying organic fertilisation only (e.g. organic agri- culture) feature lower yields than conventional ones. In con- sequence, as there is no consensus that organic fertilisation leads to increased soil C, and no agreed-upon method ac- counts for all C fluxes and stocks in soils (Benoist and Bessou 2018), total C content was retained only as an auxil- iary functional unit enabling certain comparisons (see Section 2.3).
Regarding allocation of impacts among co-products, the following strategies were followed:

<list>a. All untreated residues of agricultural origin were as- sumed to carry no environmental burden, following the AGRIBALYSE methodology and in line with the dominant practice in French LCA community. For in- stance, livestock effluents are not considered as co- products but as by-products with low value, SO no im- pact of the livestock system was allocated to the efflu- ents, but only the downstream impacts of processing and transport. Similarly, untreated sewage sludge was also assumed to have zero impact, as it is a by-product (considered as waste) from wastewater treatment; thus, only the impacts of further sludge treatment were con- sidered. Pressed cakes and husks from oily seed pro- cessing, as well as other agro-industrial residues used in COAF production, on the other hand, were instead allocated (by a mass-weighted economic key using lit- erature data) a portion of the energy and water require- ments of processing, but no impacts of their upstream agricultural phase. 
b. When waste treatments (other than phase separation) pro- duced water losses such as evaporation and lixiviation, these losses were handled as emissions, and all impacts were assigned to the resulting ROAF. 
C. In sludge treatments, thickening and dewatering of sludge were assumed to be part of the ROAF production and not of the wastewater treatment, following Pradel (2016), de- spite being a standard process in wastewater treatment plants, aimed at sludge disposal. Moreover, when a spe- cific technique is not explicitly indicated, it can be as- sumed that the LCI model of any ROAF whose treatment pathway includes thickening will include an average of thickening technologies, weighted by the amount of pop- ulation equivalents treated by each one (Richard and Pradel 2014).</list>

<list>d. When treatments such as anaerobic digestion produced also energy carriers (i.e. biogas), impacts were allocated following the rules of the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME), which recom- mends economic allocation in cases when the co- products have a different purpose or characteristic of in- terest, such as their energy content and their agronomic value. Economic allocation keys from Rehl et al. (2012) were thus retained. 
e. Background processes were extracted from ecoinvent (un- allocated or economically allocated) and AGRIBALYSE, including agricultural substrates. These processes include infrastructure (e.g. storage), transport (e.g. waste collec- tion), energy (electricity, fuels) and agricultural residues (e.g. straw). All N fertiliser inputs are allocated among all crops in a rotation for AGRIBALYSE crops. 
f. A final allocation-like consideration was the modelling of water separated during sewage sludge treatments. These water emissions were modelled as "wastewater to river", because if modelled as "water to river", a bonus (i.e. a negative emission) would be estimated and the impacts of these treatments consequently reduced. These separated waters feature a non-negligible solid matter load, and thus can be considered as wastewater.</list>

The ILCD-recommended collection of life cycle impact assessment methods (EC-JRC 2012), ILCD 2011 Midpoint+ v1.10, as implemented in SimaPro v.8.5.2 (which includes a single score but no aggregation into areas of protection), was used to estimate the production impacts of all modelled ROAF, in order to enable comparisons among them and against previously published results, of both organic and syn- thetic fertilisers. The method includes 16 midpoint impact categories: climate change (IPCC 2007), ozone depletion, hu- man toxicity-cancer effects (USEtox), human toxicity-non- cancer effects (USEtox), particulate matter, ionising radiation-human health, ionising radiation-ecosystems, photo- chemical ozone formation, acidification, terrestrial eutrophi- cation, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity (USEtox), land use (soil organic mat- ter), and mineral, fossil, and renewable resource depletion. It computes the single score applying equal weighting to all impact categories. The characterisation factor for climate change of "CO2 in air" was updated from - 1 to 0 (carbon- neutral instead of sequestration), because it was considered that CO2 in air absorbed by plants and re-released in the short term should not be treated as a sequestration. Single scores were retained because they allow harnessing the comparative power of LCA in the simplest, more accessible to non-expert fashion. Moreover, in this work, the comparative impact in- tensity is more relevant than absolute assessments.
AGRIBALYSE v1.3 and ecoinvent v3.4 were used as sources of background inventory data. Foreground data was

obtained mainly from scientific (articles) and grey literature (books, project reports, unpublished datasets), in only rough detail, and thus, the resulting life cycle impact assessment results are referred to as a "screening" LCA (Wenzel 1998). The dominant selection criteria were availability and geographical/technological pertinence. The only primary data collected were those pertaining to COAF, provided by anon- ymous producers, as well as a few instances of ROAF. The resulting inventories, as well as their comparative im- pacts, were compared with literature values and subject to vetting by French experts (i.e. the co-authors of the accompa- nying data paper Avadí et al. (2020)) on the environmental assessment of the different modelled treatment routes.

The method includes 16 midpoint impact categories: climate change (IPCC 2007), ozone depletion, hu- man toxicity-cancer effects (USEtox), human toxicity-non- cancer effects (USEtox), particulate matter, ionising radiation-human health, ionising radiation-ecosystems, photo- chemical ozone formation, acidification, terrestrial eutrophi- cation, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity (USEtox), land use (soil organic mat- ter), and mineral, fossil, and renewable resource depletion. It computes the single score applying equal weighting to all impact categories. The characterisation factor for climate change of "CO2 in air" was updated from - 1 to 0 (carbon- neutral instead of sequestration), because it was considered that CO2 in air absorbed by plants and re-released in the short term should not be treated as a sequestration. Single scores were retained because they allow harnessing the comparative power of LCA in the simplest, more accessible to non-expert fashion. Moreover, in this work, the comparative impact in- tensity is more relevant than absolute assessments.
AGRIBALYSE v1.3 and ecoinvent v3.4 were used as sources of background inventory data. Foreground data was

obtained mainly from scientific (articles) and grey literature (books, project reports, unpublished datasets), in only rough detail, and thus, the resulting life cycle impact assessment results are referred to as a "screening" LCA (Wenzel 1998). The dominant selection criteria were availability and geographical/technological pertinence. The only primary data collected were those pertaining to COAF, provided by anon- ymous producers, as well as a few instances of ROAF. The resulting inventories, as well as their comparative im- pacts, were compared with literature values and subject to vetting by French experts (i.e. the co-authors of the accompa- nying data paper Avadí et al. (2020)) on the environmental assessment of the different modelled treatment routes.
22: <header>2.1 Goal and scope </header>

The main goal of the study is to compare the impacts of pro- ducing fertilisers from organic residues, excluding their use as amendments or fertilisers (see Section 2.2.1), in a French and European context. Therefore, the discussion of direct field emissions-associated with a crop's N-balance (Bockstaller and Girardin 2010; COMIFER 2013)-delayed release of nu- trients in plant-available form, and the long-term effects of repeated ROAF/COAF application on soil quality exceed the scope of this work, and will be addressed in future publi- cations (under preparation). This study aims at informing both AGRIBALYSE users and future modelling on the rationale behind organic amendments and fertilisers LCIs developed for AGRIBALYSE.

The life cycle of organic residues recycled in agricultural soils encompasses the generation, treatment and spreading of said residues (Fig. 1, System A). Sometimes, the generation and treatment of organic residues take place in the same sys- tem, such as the case of farms treating their animal effluents and harvest residues to be used in the same farm or in others (Fig. 1, System B). Nonetheless, very often the generation,
