organic residues

Generation co-products of organic Storage|Generation co-products of organic Storage||||Transport System C1: Production of organic residues (including storage)|treatment Transport Pre- Intermediate products Treatment Intermediate and Reception storage Transport storage step n n|treatment Transport Pre- Intermediate products Treatment Intermediate and Reception storage Transport storage step n n||Transport Storage|Transport System B: generation and (partial or full) treatment|System production chemicals) (transportation, C2: Treatment of organic storage, amendments of organic direct emissions, residues and fertilisers and fuels,|System production chemicals) (transportation, C2: Treatment of organic storage, amendments of organic direct emissions, residues and fertilisers and fuels,|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct|System emissions, (transportation, amendments amendments C3: Spreading spreading) Use and of storage, organic and of fertilisers organic fertilisers direct
System A: generation, treatment and use

treatment and use of organic residues take place under differ- ent systems at different locations (Fig. 1, System C). In this study, the scope of the inventories includes the pro- vision of substrates (i.e. organic residues without commercial value and featuring no or few impacts, and organic co- products with commercial value featuring impacts from a co-production), transportation and storage (before, during and after processing), and treatments (Fig. 1, System C2). It is a gate-to-gate scope, which excludes the production and initial storage of organic residues and co-products (Fig. 1, System C1), as well as transportation to the receiving farm, storage and spreading (Fig. 1, System C3).

Moreover, following the "action-flow-stock ontology" (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Soulié and Wassenaar 2017), it is envisaged that the system models proposed in this work would be used as building blocks for agricultural LCAs where the treatment of organic residues may constitute a separate system than those generating and using them. The proposed approach would thus allow LCA modelling of different types of sys- items: storage immediate to the generation of residues (Fig. 1, System C1); treatment of residues (Fig. 1, System C2); storage immediate to the generation and (partial or full) treatment of residues (Fig. 1, System B); storage previous to the use of organic amendments and fertilisers (Fig. 1, System C3); and integrated systems including storage immediate to the gener- ation, treatment, transportation and storage previous to use (Fig. 1, System A). The generation of raw organic residues and use of treatment products is completely beyond the scope of this work, and would require additional LCI models/ building blocks.

Two design elements were particularly troublesome, name- ly, the choice of a functional unit or units enabling fair com- parisons, and the allocation of treatment co-products (espe- cially of treatments such as anaerobic digestion, which yield economically interesting co-products).

The LCIs were constructed using 1 t fresh mass of final product, complemented with 1 t of N, P, and K in fresh mass as the main functional units, but additional functional units were retained for comparisons, as detailed in Section 2.3: 1 t of each nutrient in forms readily available to the plants and 1 t of C, both in product fresh mass. The rationale behind all these functional units is that the provision of nutrients to crops is assumed to be the main driver for the production of both organic and mineral fertilisers. There are of course cases, such as in viticulture, where the main driver for the use of ROAF/ COAF may be to improve soil properties (i.e. amendment rather than fertilisation). All flows are represented in terms of fresh mass, instead of dry matter, because final users of ROAF/COAF apply them to soils after estimating their nutri- ents or organic matter contents in terms of fresh mass. Composition data was moreover compiled for all modelled ROAF and COAF (Avadí et al. 2020), and determined that the water content of these products is highly variable (i.e. it is distributed along a wide range), and thus expressing invento- ries in terms of dry matter would disregard variability and, ultimately, uncertainty.

Organic fertilisation adds organic carbon to soils, and it has been observed that organically-fertilised soil tends to feature higher soil organic matter (Tuomisto et al. 2012; Vicente- Vicente et al. 2016). Such increase in soil organic matter (i.e.

C) associated with organic fertilisation has been demonstrated, for instance, by the French long-term field experiment QualiAgro (Peltre et al. 2012; Obriot et al. 2016). Nonetheless, the capability of long-term organic fertilisation to sequester more organic C in soils than conventional (i.e. mineral or organo-mineral) fertilisation has been called into question by Kirchmann et al. (2016), with the argument that the main contributor to soil organic C is crop biomass, and that systems applying organic fertilisation only (e.g. organic agri- culture) feature lower yields than conventional ones. In con- sequence, as there is no consensus that organic fertilisation leads to increased soil C, and no agreed-upon method ac- counts for all C fluxes and stocks in soils (Benoist and Bessou 2018), total C content was retained only as an auxil- iary functional unit enabling certain comparisons (see Section 2.3).
Regarding allocation of impacts among co-products, the following strategies were followed:

<list>a. All untreated residues of agricultural origin were as- sumed to carry no environmental burden, following the AGRIBALYSE methodology and in line with the dominant practice in French LCA community. For in- stance, livestock effluents are not considered as co- products but as by-products with low value, SO no im- pact of the livestock system was allocated to the efflu- ents, but only the downstream impacts of processing and transport. Similarly, untreated sewage sludge was also assumed to have zero impact, as it is a by-product (considered as waste) from wastewater treatment; thus, only the impacts of further sludge treatment were con- sidered. Pressed cakes and husks from oily seed pro- cessing, as well as other agro-industrial residues used in COAF production, on the other hand, were instead allocated (by a mass-weighted economic key using lit- erature data) a portion of the energy and water require- ments of processing, but no impacts of their upstream agricultural phase. 
b. When waste treatments (other than phase separation) pro- duced water losses such as evaporation and lixiviation, these losses were handled as emissions, and all impacts were assigned to the resulting ROAF. 
C. In sludge treatments, thickening and dewatering of sludge were assumed to be part of the ROAF production and not of the wastewater treatment, following Pradel (2016), de- spite being a standard process in wastewater treatment plants, aimed at sludge disposal. Moreover, when a spe- cific technique is not explicitly indicated, it can be as- sumed that the LCI model of any ROAF whose treatment pathway includes thickening will include an average of thickening technologies, weighted by the amount of pop- ulation equivalents treated by each one (Richard and Pradel 2014).</list>

<list>d. When treatments such as anaerobic digestion produced also energy carriers (i.e. biogas), impacts were allocated following the rules of the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME), which recom- mends economic allocation in cases when the co- products have a different purpose or characteristic of in- terest, such as their energy content and their agronomic value. Economic allocation keys from Rehl et al. (2012) were thus retained. 
e. Background processes were extracted from ecoinvent (un- allocated or economically allocated) and AGRIBALYSE, including agricultural substrates. These processes include infrastructure (e.g. storage), transport (e.g. waste collec- tion), energy (electricity, fuels) and agricultural residues (e.g. straw). All N fertiliser inputs are allocated among all crops in a rotation for AGRIBALYSE crops. 
f. A final allocation-like consideration was the modelling of water separated during sewage sludge treatments. These water emissions were modelled as "wastewater to river", because if modelled as "water to river", a bonus (i.e. a negative emission) would be estimated and the impacts of these treatments consequently reduced. These separated waters feature a non-negligible solid matter load, and thus can be considered as wastewater.</list>

The ILCD-recommended collection of life cycle impact assessment methods (EC-JRC 2012), ILCD 2011 Midpoint+ v1.10, as implemented in SimaPro v.8.5.2 (which includes a single score but no aggregation into areas of protection), was used to estimate the production impacts of all modelled ROAF, in order to enable comparisons among them and against previously published results, of both organic and syn- thetic fertilisers. It computes the single score applying equal weighting to all impact categories. The characterisation factor for climate change of "CO2 in air" was updated from - 1 to 0 (carbon- neutral instead of sequestration), because it was considered that CO2 in air absorbed by plants and re-released in the short term should not be treated as a sequestration. Single scores were retained because they allow harnessing the comparative power of LCA in the simplest, more accessible to non-expert fashion. Moreover, in this work, the comparative impact in- tensity is more relevant than absolute assessments.

Moreover, when a spe- cific technique is not explicitly indicated, it can be as- sumed that the LCI model of any ROAF whose treatment pathway includes thickening will include an average of thickening technologies, weighted by the amount of pop- ulation equivalents treated by each one (Richard and Pradel 2014).</list>

<list>d. When treatments such as anaerobic digestion produced also energy carriers (i.e. biogas), impacts were allocated following the rules of the French Environment and Energy Management Agency (ADEME), which recom- mends economic allocation in cases when the co- products have a different purpose or characteristic of in- terest, such as their energy content and their agronomic value. Economic allocation keys from Rehl et al. (2012) were thus retained. 
e. Background processes were extracted from ecoinvent (un- allocated or economically allocated) and AGRIBALYSE, including agricultural substrates. These processes include infrastructure (e.g. storage), transport (e.g. waste collec- tion), energy (electricity, fuels) and agricultural residues (e.g. straw). All N fertiliser inputs are allocated among all crops in a rotation for AGRIBALYSE crops. 
f. A final allocation-like consideration was the modelling of water separated during sewage sludge treatments. These water emissions were modelled as "wastewater to river", because if modelled as "water to river", a bonus (i.e. a negative emission) would be estimated and the impacts of these treatments consequently reduced. These separated waters feature a non-negligible solid matter load, and thus can be considered as wastewater.</list>

The ILCD-recommended collection of life cycle impact assessment methods (EC-JRC 2012), ILCD 2011 Midpoint+ v1.10, as implemented in SimaPro v.8.5.2 (which includes a single score but no aggregation into areas of protection), was used to estimate the production impacts of all modelled ROAF, in order to enable comparisons among them and against previously published results, of both organic and syn- thetic fertilisers. The method includes 16 midpoint impact categories: climate change (IPCC 2007), ozone depletion, hu- man toxicity-cancer effects (USEtox), human toxicity-non- cancer effects (USEtox), particulate matter, ionising radiation-human health, ionising radiation-ecosystems, photo- chemical ozone formation, acidification, terrestrial eutrophi- cation, freshwater eutrophication, marine eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity (USEtox), land use (soil organic mat- ter), and mineral, fossil, and renewable resource depletion. It computes the single score applying equal weighting to all impact categories. The characterisation factor for climate change of "CO2 in air" was updated from - 1 to 0 (carbon- neutral instead of sequestration), because it was considered that CO2 in air absorbed by plants and re-released in the short term should not be treated as a sequestration. Single scores were retained because they allow harnessing the comparative power of LCA in the simplest, more accessible to non-expert fashion. Moreover, in this work, the comparative impact in- tensity is more relevant than absolute assessments.
AGRIBALYSE v1.3 and ecoinvent v3.4 were used as sources of background inventory data. Foreground data was

obtained mainly from scientific (articles) and grey literature (books, project reports, unpublished datasets), in only rough detail, and thus, the resulting life cycle impact assessment results are referred to as a "screening" LCA (Wenzel 1998). The dominant selection criteria were availability and geographical/technological pertinence. The only primary data collected were those pertaining to COAF, provided by anon- ymous producers, as well as a few instances of ROAF. The resulting inventories, as well as their comparative im- pacts, were compared with literature values and subject to vetting by French experts (i.e. the co-authors of the accompa- nying data paper Avadí et al. (2020)) on the environmental assessment of the different modelled treatment routes.
25: <header>2.2.1 Classification of treatment products as amendments or fertilisers </header>

Based on the official French classification (Avadí et al. 2020), poultry manure, rendered animal by-products (i.e. animal meals from blood, meat, bones, feathers, and offal), press cakes, slurry-based digestates-and in general most raw digestates and their liquid phase (ADEME 2011a)-and enriched composts (such as COAF), are classified as fertilisers, while simple composts (ROAF) and raw solid ma- nure as amendments. The lack of specific examples regarding raw slurry as well as limed, dewatered, and dried sludge- which in France are subject to a spreading plan-forced us to classify these materials by ourselves, following the listed criteria and expert opinions. A key criterion is the content of total N, with values below 3% in fresh mass for amendments and (generally) above for fertilisers. Other dominating criteria for amendments are dry matter content, which must be over 30%, as well as the C/N ratio (> and the content of organic matter 20). Based on these criteria, but also on the purpose associated with the use of these products, they were classified as shown in Table 2.

Sewage sludge in particular is (still) overwhelmingly consid- ered in France and Europe, a priori, as a waste (Houot et al. 2014). To be legally considered as product, a sludge-based prod- uct must satisfy a demand, fulfil a specific function, and respect technical and legal requirements, and do not contribute to nega- tive environmental and human health impacts (Pradel 2017). Such departure from the waste status is being addressed, at the European level, through the End-of-Waste Directive and other legislation (http://ec.europa.eu/environment/waste/sludge/index. htm). In France, treated sewage sludge can be considered a product on a case-by-case basis, after approval by ANSES or standardisation by AFNOR.

<list>Sewage sludge and digestates release a part of mineral and organic N in the 1st year of application, unlike composts, and thus can be in general considered fertilisers (with ex- ceptions detailed below). 
Dewatered, dried, and digested sludge are amply used as fertilisers, and limed sludge as amendment (ADEME 2016). Digested sludge, be it dewatered and/or pelletised, can be considered fertiliser, as it will always release N and does not contain any fibres. Sludge co-composted with a fibre-rich substrate can be considered amendments (e.g. compost of sludge + green waste). 
The solid phase of digestates from livestock effluents can be considered as an amendment (there are still a lot of fibres left in the solid fractions), and the liquid phase as a fertiliser. The same applies if the phases are dried and/or pelletised. In general, the solid phase of digestates (other than sludge), be it composted or not, can be classified as amendments. 
Solid digestates (40-50% dry matter) can be considered as amendments, yet of fertilising interest. 
The final effluent from aerobic treatment of slurry consists of a K-loaded liquid, with some N, and can be considered a fertiliser. 
Raw poultry manure, with and without litter, can be con- sidered a fertiliser, as it consists of a nutrient-laden product.</list>
Table 2 Ad hoc classification of residue-based products not offi- cially classified as amendments or fertilisers

Products|N (%)|C/N|Organic matter (% of fresh mass)|Dry matter (%)|Classification Limed sewage sludge|0.6-0.9|8-11|8-20|25-40|Amendment (basic) Dewatered sewage sludge|0.8-1.2|5-6|8-15|16-22|Fertiliser Dried sewage sludge|3-4|4-6|45-67|90-95|Fertiliser Dewatered, dried, or digested sewage sludge|3-6|6-12|50-64|27-90|Fertiliser Raw sewage sludge|6-41|5-8|1.3-5|1-5|Fertiliser Raw slurry|0.2-0.5|4.5-7|11-14|5-7.6|Fertiliser
Sources: SOLAGRO (2001), Solagro-IRSTEA (2012), Benoît et al. (2014), and Houot et al. (2014)

29: The following organic wastes were considered for ROAF: liquid (slurry) and solid livestock manure; agro-industrial res- idues such as wool, coffee, cacao, oil seeds, and cereal frac- tions, and animal meals, straw, and other harvest residues; wastewater treatment sludge; urban green waste; and biowaste. The treatments and treatment steps modelled were (Fig. 2) stocking in silos and on concrete surfaces; substrate shredding and sieving; substrate mixing; anaerobic and aero- bic treatments; thickening, dewatering (dehydration), drying, and pelletising; phase separating liquid fractions and various biological and physicochemical treatments such as ammonia stripping, liming, and flocculation.
30: <header>2.2.3 Commercial organic amendments and fertiliser production </header>

The pathway leading to "O - Commercial organic amend- ments and fertilisers" in Fig. 2 depicts the industrial produc- tion of solid COAF (the production of liquid fertilisers was not modelled). Various firms producing such products exist in France, with overall annual deliveries of 0.398 Mt (Table 1), of which ~ 50% are consumed by viticulture and up to 10% by

certified organic agriculture. Organic agriculture (labelled as Agriculture Biologique (AB) in France) is highly regulated in Europe, and is characterised by management practices that completely excludes the use of synthetic fertilisers (http:// agriculture.gouv.fr/lagriculture-biologique-1). The modelled products consist of a base of composted agricultural and agro-industrial residues, at which additional plant and animal materials are added to regulate the N:P:K ratio. At least one large French producer includes a rendered fish residue-based input, which was not modelled. When synthetic N substances are added, the final mixture is labelled as organo-mineral fertiliser. One of the largest French producers, representing ~ 18% of total production, provided primary data.
31: When available, AGRIBALYSE processes were preferred for background data, mainly to guarantee methodological consis- tency. For instance, the impacts of French wheat straw from the Agri-footprint database (Blonk Agri-footprint 2014) are dominated by direct field emissions, and are more than double of those of the average wheat straw from AGRIBALYSE, which is dominated by transportation activities. This differ- ence mainly results from the fact that Agri-footprint allocated

23% of wheat production impacts to straw, whereas Agribalyse allocated no impacts from cereal production to straw. The ecoinvent wheat straw processes are valid mainly for Switzerland, and their generic global straw market pro- cesses are dominated by international cereal production. Wheat straw from AGRIBALYSE was used as a proxy for both "straw" and "green waste", given that the impacts of both residues correspond to their transportation and shredding.

All digestates including agricultural inputs (slurry, manure, crops such as maize silage, agricultural residues) are referred to as "agricultural digestates". The average agricultural digestate was modelled as 66% average digestate of livestock effluents, 14% of digestate from maize silage, and 20% of average digestate from mixed livestock effluents and maize silage, based on the data in Houot et al. (2014). The average digestate of livestock effluents consists of 40% cattle slurry, 40% of swine slurry, and 20% of mixed slurry, while the average digestate from mixed livestock effluents and maize silage was assumed to consist of equal parts of digestates of two representative proportions of slurry and maize silage (5:95 and 19:81), based on ADEME (2011b).
To represent "biowaste", an ecoinvent process representing the collection of municipal solid waste, complemented with a shredding process, was retained.

Data for modelling the processing of agro-industrial resi- dues used in COAF production, in terms of energy and water consumption, were sourced from literature. These inputs in- cluded coffee processing residues (Franca and Oliveira 2009; Humbert et al. 2009), shredded cocoa shells (Perez Neira 2016; Recanati et al. 2018), shredded olive pomace (Intini et al. 2011; Tsarouhas et al. 2015; Schau et al. 2016), wool residues from wool scouring and skirting (Macpherson 2012; Moreira Cardoso 2013), and animal meals (Gooding 2012). Economic data for allocation were obtained, from the listed sources, for wool, cocoa, and olive residues only. For coffee

residues, no scientific or grey literature source was found, SO a similar economic allocation key as for olive and cocoa resi- dues was applied. Life cycle inventories for these materials are presented in the accompanying Data in Brief article (Avadí et al. 2020).

38: The following comparisons among impact assessment indica- tors corresponding to the various modelled ROAF and COAF, consolidated into a single score, were performed:

<list>The main (in terms of annual quantities produced) amend- ments, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic fertilisers, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic amendments and fertilisers were com- pared, across groups, in terms of their IOMS, MFE, and KeqN, the former being an indicator of the amending val- ue of ROAF/COAF and the two latter being indicators of N "quality" (which tend to converge for most organic amendments and fertilisers). These indicators are useful devices to compare the agronomic relevance of ROAF and COAF. IOMS2 stands for "index of organic matter stability", representing the proportion of organic matter that will likely contribute to the replenishment of C stocks in soil (i.e. the percentage of organic matter in the added product estimated to be present in the soil approximately 1 year after application). It is computed from the biochem- ical soluble, cellulosic, and lignin-like fractions of organic matter in ROAF/COAF and the proportion of rapidly</list>
2 Referred to among French institutions and researchers as "indice de stabilité de la matière organique (ISMO)".
Fig. 4 Contribution analysis of the main substrates used in organic fertilisers produced in France, corresponding to the single score per 1 t fresh matter

mineralisable C. Details and calculated values are provided in Lashermes et al. (2009) and Fuchs et al. (2014). MFE stands for "mineral fertiliser equivalent", a measure of the capability of organic fertilisers to substitute mineral ones, based on their content of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N-i.e. readily available for the plants-(Brockmann et al. 2018). It conveys the residual fertilising value of organic fertilisers after NO3 and NH3 emissions. It is calculated as "first year" and "long term", based on short- and long-term N mineralisation rates (thus, the latter includes delayed release of mineralised N). MFE calculation means and data are provided in Brockmann et al. (2018). KeqN (of the crop cycle) represents a coef- ficient of N fertiliser equivalence (COMIFER 2013), representing the ratio between the amount of N provided

<list>by a mineral fertiliser of the ammonium nitrate type and the total amount of N provided by an organic fertiliser which allows the same N absorption by the crop over its cycle. It depends on the type of crop and its conditions (rotation, seasonality). KeqN details and calculated values are provided in COMIFER (2013). It is assumed that the mineralisation of N converges in the long term towards that of soil humus (soil organic matter).

No example crops from outside France (e.g. tropical) were retained for comparison, because both MFE and KeqN are dependent on pedo-climatic condi- tions, and the required data required for non-temperate KeqN calculations was not available.

The use of MFE or KeqN as functional units for comparing organic and mineral fertilisers can be criticised. For instance, as system equilibrium conditions are assumed in LCA, it can be assumed that, in the long term, all N brought by organic fertilisers would be available to crops, and thus, each total nutrient present in organic fertilisers should be taken into con- sideration (total N, P205, K20, and, eventually, IOMS). A counterargument is that considering total N would be prob- lematic, as it undergoes losses and assimilation of mineral N

Fig. Comparative single scores for the amounts of selected organic fertilisers produced in France required fertilising 1 ha of wheat and of conventional mineral fertilisers, per various functional units. MFE, mineral fertiliser equivalent; KeqN, coefficient of N fertiliser equivalence

by microorganisms in the substrate 3 at different rates for or- ganic and mineral fertilisers. Total N, MFE (long-term), and KeqN (mean of reported values) were retained to compare the delivery of N by both organic and mineral fertilisers, the latter featuring MFE = KeqN = 1.
Total P and K contents of ROAF/COAF were occasionally reported in terms of P2O5 and K2O and otherwise adjusted by the conversion factors 0.436 (P:P2O5) and 0.830 (K:K2O).

A more comprehensive comparison of organic and mineral fertilisers, beyond comparing the impacts of their production, would require (for instance, in the context of regional assess- ments) the consideration of alternative pathways of organic waste disposal. Such expansion of the system boundary would also expand the function delivered by the system: from the delivery of fertilising units only to the delivery of fertilising units plus the disposal of organic waste. This comparison was performed for a few selected ROAF (excluding direct emis- sions from fertiliser use), in terms of the amounts of materials required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat.

The selection of products was based on French production and consumption statistics. To be able to compare products produced by different pathways and technologies, scaling factors were calculated, based on the nutrient content of each fertiliser, to express all ROAF/COAF per t N, P, P2O5, K, or K2O in fresh mass. To calculate MFE values, the Excel tool associated with Brockmann et al. (2018) was used.
Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses were performed as well: sensitivity was based on the energy intensity of

treatments (i.e. contrasting extreme values reported in the lit- erature) and on the allocation of production impacts to agri- cultural residues (part of the co-production impacts allocated to residues, instead of zero impact). Data uncertainty was propagated with Monte Carlo, across ROAF (data paucity prevented performing uncertainty analyses on COAF).
Finally, selected midpoint results were compared with those from previous studies.
39: The following comparisons among impact assessment indica- tors corresponding to the various modelled ROAF and COAF, consolidated into a single score, were performed:

<list>The main (in terms of annual quantities produced) amend- ments, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic fertilisers, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic amendments and fertilisers were com- pared, across groups, in terms of their IOMS, MFE, and KeqN, the former being an indicator of the amending val- ue of ROAF/COAF and the two latter being indicators of N "quality" (which tend to converge for most organic amendments and fertilisers). These indicators are useful devices to compare the agronomic relevance of ROAF and COAF. IOMS2 stands for "index of organic matter stability", representing the proportion of organic matter that will likely contribute to the replenishment of C stocks in soil (i.e. the percentage of organic matter in the added product estimated to be present in the soil approximately 1 year after application). It is computed from the biochem- ical soluble, cellulosic, and lignin-like fractions of organic matter in ROAF/COAF and the proportion of rapidly</list>

mineralisable C. Details and calculated values are provided in Lashermes et al. (2009) and Fuchs et al. (2014). MFE stands for "mineral fertiliser equivalent", a measure of the capability of organic fertilisers to substitute mineral ones, based on their content of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N-i.e. readily available for the plants-(Brockmann et al. 2018). It conveys the residual fertilising value of organic fertilisers after NO3 and NH3 emissions. It is calculated as "first year" and "long term", based on short- and long-term N mineralisation rates (thus, the latter includes delayed release of mineralised N). MFE calculation means and data are provided in Brockmann et al. (2018). KeqN (of the crop cycle) represents a coef- ficient of N fertiliser equivalence (COMIFER 2013), representing the ratio between the amount of N provided

<list>by a mineral fertiliser of the ammonium nitrate type and the total amount of N provided by an organic fertiliser which allows the same N absorption by the crop over its cycle. It depends on the type of crop and its conditions (rotation, seasonality). KeqN details and calculated values are provided in COMIFER (2013). It is assumed that the mineralisation of N converges in the long term towards that of soil humus (soil organic matter).

8000 kg/ha, at 15% humidity, being preceded by wheat, grown on a soil receiving regular applications of P and K, and assuming all N needs are met by the input fertiliser (https://fertilisation-edu.fr/cultures-fiches-pratiques/ble. html). No example crops from outside France (e.g. tropical) were retained for comparison, because both MFE and KeqN are dependent on pedo-climatic condi- tions, and the required data required for non-temperate KeqN calculations was not available.

The use of MFE or KeqN as functional units for comparing organic and mineral fertilisers can be criticised. For instance, as system equilibrium conditions are assumed in LCA, it can be assumed that, in the long term, all N brought by organic fertilisers would be available to crops, and thus, each total nutrient present in organic fertilisers should be taken into con- sideration (total N, P205, K20, and, eventually, IOMS). A counterargument is that considering total N would be prob- lematic, as it undergoes losses and assimilation of mineral N

Fig. Comparative single scores for the amounts of selected organic fertilisers produced in France required fertilising 1 ha of wheat and of conventional mineral fertilisers, per various functional units. MFE, mineral fertiliser equivalent; KeqN, coefficient of N fertiliser equivalence

by microorganisms in the substrate 3 at different rates for or- ganic and mineral fertilisers. Total N, MFE (long-term), and KeqN (mean of reported values) were retained to compare the delivery of N by both organic and mineral fertilisers, the latter featuring MFE = KeqN = 1.
Total P and K contents of ROAF/COAF were occasionally reported in terms of P2O5 and K2O and otherwise adjusted by the conversion factors 0.436 (P:P2O5) and 0.830 (K:K2O).

A more comprehensive comparison of organic and mineral fertilisers, beyond comparing the impacts of their production, would require (for instance, in the context of regional assess- ments) the consideration of alternative pathways of organic waste disposal. Such expansion of the system boundary would also expand the function delivered by the system: from the delivery of fertilising units only to the delivery of fertilising units plus the disposal of organic waste. This comparison was performed for a few selected ROAF (excluding direct emis- sions from fertiliser use), in terms of the amounts of materials required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat.

The selection of products was based on French production and consumption statistics. To be able to compare products produced by different pathways and technologies, scaling factors were calculated, based on the nutrient content of each fertiliser, to express all ROAF/COAF per t N, P, P2O5, K, or K2O in fresh mass. To calculate MFE values, the Excel tool associated with Brockmann et al. (2018) was used.
Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses were performed as well: sensitivity was based on the energy intensity of

treatments (i.e. contrasting extreme values reported in the lit- erature) and on the allocation of production impacts to agri- cultural residues (part of the co-production impacts allocated to residues, instead of zero impact). Data uncertainty was propagated with Monte Carlo, across ROAF (data paucity prevented performing uncertainty analyses on COAF).
Finally, selected midpoint results were compared with those from previous studies.
41: The following comparisons among impact assessment indica- tors corresponding to the various modelled ROAF and COAF, consolidated into a single score, were performed:

<list>The main (in terms of annual quantities produced) amend- ments, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic fertilisers, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic amendments and fertilisers were com- pared, across groups, in terms of their IOMS, MFE, and KeqN, the former being an indicator of the amending val- ue of ROAF/COAF and the two latter being indicators of N "quality" (which tend to converge for most organic amendments and fertilisers). These indicators are useful devices to compare the agronomic relevance of ROAF and COAF. IOMS2 stands for "index of organic matter stability", representing the proportion of organic matter that will likely contribute to the replenishment of C stocks in soil (i.e. the percentage of organic matter in the added product estimated to be present in the soil approximately 1 year after application). It is computed from the biochem- ical soluble, cellulosic, and lignin-like fractions of organic matter in ROAF/COAF and the proportion of rapidly</list>
2 Referred to among French institutions and researchers as "indice de stabilité de la matière organique (ISMO)".
Fig. 4 Contribution analysis of the main substrates used in organic fertilisers produced in France, corresponding to the single score per 1 t fresh matter

mineralisable C. Details and calculated values are provided in Lashermes et al. (2009) and Fuchs et al. (2014). MFE stands for "mineral fertiliser equivalent", a measure of the capability of organic fertilisers to substitute mineral ones, based on their content of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N-i.e. readily available for the plants-(Brockmann et al. 2018). It conveys the residual fertilising value of organic fertilisers after NO3 and NH3 emissions. It is calculated as "first year" and "long term", based on short- and long-term N mineralisation rates (thus, the latter includes delayed release of mineralised N). MFE calculation means and data are provided in Brockmann et al. (2018). KeqN (of the crop cycle) represents a coef- ficient of N fertiliser equivalence (COMIFER 2013), representing the ratio between the amount of N provided

<list>by a mineral fertiliser of the ammonium nitrate type and the total amount of N provided by an organic fertiliser which allows the same N absorption by the crop over its cycle. It depends on the type of crop and its conditions (rotation, seasonality). KeqN details and calculated values are provided in COMIFER (2013). It is assumed that the mineralisation of N converges in the long term towards that of soil humus (soil organic matter). 
The organic fertilisers with the highest nutrient contents were compared with the most common mineral N, P, and K fertilisers available in ecoinvent, per of N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O in fresh mass, as well as in terms of the amount of each product required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat-i.e. 280 kg total N, 83 kg P2O5, and 48 kg K2O, assuming a French crop, with an expected yield of</list>

by microorganisms in the substrate 3 at different rates for or- ganic and mineral fertilisers. Total N, MFE (long-term), and KeqN (mean of reported values) were retained to compare the delivery of N by both organic and mineral fertilisers, the latter featuring MFE = KeqN = 1.
Total P and K contents of ROAF/COAF were occasionally reported in terms of P2O5 and K2O and otherwise adjusted by the conversion factors 0.436 (P:P2O5) and 0.830 (K:K2O).

A more comprehensive comparison of organic and mineral fertilisers, beyond comparing the impacts of their production, would require (for instance, in the context of regional assess- ments) the consideration of alternative pathways of organic waste disposal. Such expansion of the system boundary would also expand the function delivered by the system: from the delivery of fertilising units only to the delivery of fertilising units plus the disposal of organic waste. This comparison was performed for a few selected ROAF (excluding direct emis- sions from fertiliser use), in terms of the amounts of materials required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat.

The selection of products was based on French production and consumption statistics. To be able to compare products produced by different pathways and technologies, scaling factors were calculated, based on the nutrient content of each fertiliser, to express all ROAF/COAF per t N, P, P2O5, K, or K2O in fresh mass. To calculate MFE values, the Excel tool associated with Brockmann et al. (2018) was used.
Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses were performed as well: sensitivity was based on the energy intensity of

treatments (i.e. contrasting extreme values reported in the lit- erature) and on the allocation of production impacts to agri- cultural residues (part of the co-production impacts allocated to residues, instead of zero impact). Data uncertainty was propagated with Monte Carlo, across ROAF (data paucity prevented performing uncertainty analyses on COAF).
Finally, selected midpoint results were compared with those from previous studies.

MFE stands for "mineral fertiliser equivalent", a measure of the capability of organic fertilisers to substitute mineral ones, based on their content of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N-i.e. readily available for the plants-(Brockmann et al. 2018). No example crops from outside France (e.g. tropical) were retained for comparison, because both MFE and KeqN are dependent on pedo-climatic condi- tions, and the required data required for non-temperate KeqN calculations was not available.

The use of MFE or KeqN as functional units for comparing organic and mineral fertilisers can be criticised. For instance, as system equilibrium conditions are assumed in LCA, it can be assumed that, in the long term, all N brought by organic fertilisers would be available to crops, and thus, each total nutrient present in organic fertilisers should be taken into con- sideration (total N, P205, K20, and, eventually, IOMS). A counterargument is that considering total N would be prob- lematic, as it undergoes losses and assimilation of mineral N

Fig. Comparative single scores for the amounts of selected organic fertilisers produced in France required fertilising 1 ha of wheat and of conventional mineral fertilisers, per various functional units. MFE, mineral fertiliser equivalent; KeqN, coefficient of N fertiliser equivalence

by microorganisms in the substrate 3 at different rates for or- ganic and mineral fertilisers. Total N, MFE (long-term), and KeqN (mean of reported values) were retained to compare the delivery of N by both organic and mineral fertilisers, the latter featuring MFE = KeqN = 1.
Total P and K contents of ROAF/COAF were occasionally reported in terms of P2O5 and K2O and otherwise adjusted by the conversion factors 0.436 (P:P2O5) and 0.830 (K:K2O).

A more comprehensive comparison of organic and mineral fertilisers, beyond comparing the impacts of their production, would require (for instance, in the context of regional assess- ments) the consideration of alternative pathways of organic waste disposal. Such expansion of the system boundary would also expand the function delivered by the system: from the delivery of fertilising units only to the delivery of fertilising units plus the disposal of organic waste. This comparison was performed for a few selected ROAF (excluding direct emis- sions from fertiliser use), in terms of the amounts of materials required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat.

The selection of products was based on French production and consumption statistics. To be able to compare products produced by different pathways and technologies, scaling factors were calculated, based on the nutrient content of each fertiliser, to express all ROAF/COAF per t N, P, P2O5, K, or K2O in fresh mass. To calculate MFE values, the Excel tool associated with Brockmann et al. (2018) was used.
Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses were performed as well: sensitivity was based on the energy intensity of

treatments (i.e. contrasting extreme values reported in the lit- erature) and on the allocation of production impacts to agri- cultural residues (part of the co-production impacts allocated to residues, instead of zero impact). Data uncertainty was propagated with Monte Carlo, across ROAF (data paucity prevented performing uncertainty analyses on COAF).
Finally, selected midpoint results were compared with those from previous studies.
46: The following comparisons among impact assessment indica- tors corresponding to the various modelled ROAF and COAF, consolidated into a single score, were performed:

<list>The main (in terms of annual quantities produced) amend- ments, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic fertilisers, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic amendments and fertilisers were com- pared, across groups, in terms of their IOMS, MFE, and KeqN, the former being an indicator of the amending val- ue of ROAF/COAF and the two latter being indicators of N "quality" (which tend to converge for most organic amendments and fertilisers). These indicators are useful devices to compare the agronomic relevance of ROAF and COAF. IOMS2 stands for "index of organic matter stability", representing the proportion of organic matter that will likely contribute to the replenishment of C stocks in soil (i.e. the percentage of organic matter in the added product estimated to be present in the soil approximately 1 year after application). It is computed from the biochem- ical soluble, cellulosic, and lignin-like fractions of organic matter in ROAF/COAF and the proportion of rapidly</list>

mineralisable C. Details and calculated values are provided in Lashermes et al. (2009) and Fuchs et al. (2014). MFE stands for "mineral fertiliser equivalent", a measure of the capability of organic fertilisers to substitute mineral ones, based on their content of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N-i.e. readily available for the plants-(Brockmann et al. 2018). It conveys the residual fertilising value of organic fertilisers after NO3 and NH3 emissions. It is calculated as "first year" and "long term", based on short- and long-term N mineralisation rates (thus, the latter includes delayed release of mineralised N). MFE calculation means and data are provided in Brockmann et al. (2018). KeqN (of the crop cycle) represents a coef- ficient of N fertiliser equivalence (COMIFER 2013), representing the ratio between the amount of N provided

<list>by a mineral fertiliser of the ammonium nitrate type and the total amount of N provided by an organic fertiliser which allows the same N absorption by the crop over its cycle. It depends on the type of crop and its conditions (rotation, seasonality). KeqN details and calculated values are provided in COMIFER (2013). It is assumed that the mineralisation of N converges in the long term towards that of soil humus (soil organic matter).

8000 kg/ha, at 15% humidity, being preceded by wheat, grown on a soil receiving regular applications of P and K, and assuming all N needs are met by the input fertiliser (https://fertilisation-edu.fr/cultures-fiches-pratiques/ble. html). No example crops from outside France (e.g. tropical) were retained for comparison, because both MFE and KeqN are dependent on pedo-climatic condi- tions, and the required data required for non-temperate KeqN calculations was not available.

The use of MFE or KeqN as functional units for comparing organic and mineral fertilisers can be criticised. For instance, as system equilibrium conditions are assumed in LCA, it can be assumed that, in the long term, all N brought by organic fertilisers would be available to crops, and thus, each total nutrient present in organic fertilisers should be taken into con- sideration (total N, P205, K20, and, eventually, IOMS). A counterargument is that considering total N would be prob- lematic, as it undergoes losses and assimilation of mineral N

Fig. Comparative single scores for the amounts of selected organic fertilisers produced in France required fertilising 1 ha of wheat and of conventional mineral fertilisers, per various functional units. MFE, mineral fertiliser equivalent; KeqN, coefficient of N fertiliser equivalence

by microorganisms in the substrate 3 at different rates for or- ganic and mineral fertilisers. Total N, MFE (long-term), and KeqN (mean of reported values) were retained to compare the delivery of N by both organic and mineral fertilisers, the latter featuring MFE = KeqN = 1.
Total P and K contents of ROAF/COAF were occasionally reported in terms of P2O5 and K2O and otherwise adjusted by the conversion factors 0.436 (P:P2O5) and 0.830 (K:K2O).

A more comprehensive comparison of organic and mineral fertilisers, beyond comparing the impacts of their production, would require (for instance, in the context of regional assess- ments) the consideration of alternative pathways of organic waste disposal. Such expansion of the system boundary would also expand the function delivered by the system: from the delivery of fertilising units only to the delivery of fertilising units plus the disposal of organic waste. This comparison was performed for a few selected ROAF (excluding direct emis- sions from fertiliser use), in terms of the amounts of materials required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat.

The selection of products was based on French production and consumption statistics. To be able to compare products produced by different pathways and technologies, scaling factors were calculated, based on the nutrient content of each fertiliser, to express all ROAF/COAF per t N, P, P2O5, K, or K2O in fresh mass. To calculate MFE values, the Excel tool associated with Brockmann et al. (2018) was used.
Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses were performed as well: sensitivity was based on the energy intensity of

treatments (i.e. contrasting extreme values reported in the lit- erature) and on the allocation of production impacts to agri- cultural residues (part of the co-production impacts allocated to residues, instead of zero impact). Data uncertainty was propagated with Monte Carlo, across ROAF (data paucity prevented performing uncertainty analyses on COAF).
Finally, selected midpoint results were compared with those from previous studies.
48: The following comparisons among impact assessment indica- tors corresponding to the various modelled ROAF and COAF, consolidated into a single score, were performed:

<list>The main (in terms of annual quantities produced) amend- ments, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic fertilisers, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic amendments and fertilisers were com- pared, across groups, in terms of their IOMS, MFE, and KeqN, the former being an indicator of the amending val- ue of ROAF/COAF and the two latter being indicators of N "quality" (which tend to converge for most organic amendments and fertilisers). These indicators are useful devices to compare the agronomic relevance of ROAF and COAF. IOMS2 stands for "index of organic matter stability", representing the proportion of organic matter that will likely contribute to the replenishment of C stocks in soil (i.e. the percentage of organic matter in the added product estimated to be present in the soil approximately 1 year after application). It is computed from the biochem- ical soluble, cellulosic, and lignin-like fractions of organic matter in ROAF/COAF and the proportion of rapidly</list>
2 Referred to among French institutions and researchers as "indice de stabilité de la matière organique (ISMO)".
Fig. 4 Contribution analysis of the main substrates used in organic fertilisers produced in France, corresponding to the single score per 1 t fresh matter

mineralisable C. Details and calculated values are provided in Lashermes et al. (2009) and Fuchs et al. (2014). MFE stands for "mineral fertiliser equivalent", a measure of the capability of organic fertilisers to substitute mineral ones, based on their content of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N-i.e. readily available for the plants-(Brockmann et al. 2018). It conveys the residual fertilising value of organic fertilisers after NO3 and NH3 emissions. It is calculated as "first year" and "long term", based on short- and long-term N mineralisation rates (thus, the latter includes delayed release of mineralised N). MFE calculation means and data are provided in Brockmann et al. (2018). KeqN (of the crop cycle) represents a coef- ficient of N fertiliser equivalence (COMIFER 2013), representing the ratio between the amount of N provided

<list>by a mineral fertiliser of the ammonium nitrate type and the total amount of N provided by an organic fertiliser which allows the same N absorption by the crop over its cycle. It depends on the type of crop and its conditions (rotation, seasonality). KeqN details and calculated values are provided in COMIFER (2013). It is assumed that the mineralisation of N converges in the long term towards that of soil humus (soil organic matter).

by microorganisms in the substrate 3 at different rates for or- ganic and mineral fertilisers. Total N, MFE (long-term), and KeqN (mean of reported values) were retained to compare the delivery of N by both organic and mineral fertilisers, the latter featuring MFE = KeqN = 1.
Total P and K contents of ROAF/COAF were occasionally reported in terms of P2O5 and K2O and otherwise adjusted by the conversion factors 0.436 (P:P2O5) and 0.830 (K:K2O).

A more comprehensive comparison of organic and mineral fertilisers, beyond comparing the impacts of their production, would require (for instance, in the context of regional assess- ments) the consideration of alternative pathways of organic waste disposal. Such expansion of the system boundary would also expand the function delivered by the system: from the delivery of fertilising units only to the delivery of fertilising units plus the disposal of organic waste. This comparison was performed for a few selected ROAF (excluding direct emis- sions from fertiliser use), in terms of the amounts of materials required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat.

The selection of products was based on French production and consumption statistics. To be able to compare products produced by different pathways and technologies, scaling factors were calculated, based on the nutrient content of each fertiliser, to express all ROAF/COAF per t N, P, P2O5, K, or K2O in fresh mass. To calculate MFE values, the Excel tool associated with Brockmann et al. (2018) was used.
Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses were performed as well: sensitivity was based on the energy intensity of

treatments (i.e. contrasting extreme values reported in the lit- erature) and on the allocation of production impacts to agri- cultural residues (part of the co-production impacts allocated to residues, instead of zero impact). Data uncertainty was propagated with Monte Carlo, across ROAF (data paucity prevented performing uncertainty analyses on COAF).
Finally, selected midpoint results were compared with those from previous studies.

The main organic amendments and fertilisers were com- pared, across groups, in terms of their IOMS, MFE, and KeqN, the former being an indicator of the amending val- ue of ROAF/COAF and the two latter being indicators of N "quality" (which tend to converge for most organic amendments and fertilisers). These indicators are useful devices to compare the agronomic relevance of ROAF and COAF. IOMS2 stands for "index of organic matter stability", representing the proportion of organic matter that will likely contribute to the replenishment of C stocks in soil (i.e. the percentage of organic matter in the added product estimated to be present in the soil approximately 1 year after application). It is computed from the biochem- ical soluble, cellulosic, and lignin-like fractions of organic matter in ROAF/COAF and the proportion of rapidly</list>

mineralisable C. Details and calculated values are provided in Lashermes et al. (2009) and Fuchs et al. (2014). MFE stands for "mineral fertiliser equivalent", a measure of the capability of organic fertilisers to substitute mineral ones, based on their content of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N-i.e. readily available for the plants-(Brockmann et al. 2018). It conveys the residual fertilising value of organic fertilisers after NO3 and NH3 emissions. It is calculated as "first year" and "long term", based on short- and long-term N mineralisation rates (thus, the latter includes delayed release of mineralised N). <list>by a mineral fertiliser of the ammonium nitrate type and the total amount of N provided by an organic fertiliser which allows the same N absorption by the crop over its cycle. It depends on the type of crop and its conditions (rotation, seasonality). KeqN details and calculated values are provided in COMIFER (2013). It is assumed that the mineralisation of N converges in the long term towards that of soil humus (soil organic matter).

No example crops from outside France (e.g. tropical) were retained for comparison, because both MFE and KeqN are dependent on pedo-climatic condi- tions, and the required data required for non-temperate KeqN calculations was not available.

The use of MFE or KeqN as functional units for comparing organic and mineral fertilisers can be criticised. For instance, as system equilibrium conditions are assumed in LCA, it can be assumed that, in the long term, all N brought by organic fertilisers would be available to crops, and thus, each total nutrient present in organic fertilisers should be taken into con- sideration (total N, P205, K20, and, eventually, IOMS). A counterargument is that considering total N would be prob- lematic, as it undergoes losses and assimilation of mineral N

by microorganisms in the substrate 3 at different rates for or- ganic and mineral fertilisers. Total N, MFE (long-term), and KeqN (mean of reported values) were retained to compare the delivery of N by both organic and mineral fertilisers, the latter featuring MFE = KeqN = 1.
Total P and K contents of ROAF/COAF were occasionally reported in terms of P2O5 and K2O and otherwise adjusted by the conversion factors 0.436 (P:P2O5) and 0.830 (K:K2O).

A more comprehensive comparison of organic and mineral fertilisers, beyond comparing the impacts of their production, would require (for instance, in the context of regional assess- ments) the consideration of alternative pathways of organic waste disposal. Such expansion of the system boundary would also expand the function delivered by the system: from the delivery of fertilising units only to the delivery of fertilising units plus the disposal of organic waste. This comparison was performed for a few selected ROAF (excluding direct emis- sions from fertiliser use), in terms of the amounts of materials required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat.

The selection of products was based on French production and consumption statistics. To be able to compare products produced by different pathways and technologies, scaling factors were calculated, based on the nutrient content of each fertiliser, to express all ROAF/COAF per t N, P, P2O5, K, or K2O in fresh mass. To calculate MFE values, the Excel tool associated with Brockmann et al. (2018) was used.
Sensitivity and uncertainty analyses were performed as well: sensitivity was based on the energy intensity of

treatments (i.e. contrasting extreme values reported in the lit- erature) and on the allocation of production impacts to agri- cultural residues (part of the co-production impacts allocated to residues, instead of zero impact). Data uncertainty was propagated with Monte Carlo, across ROAF (data paucity prevented performing uncertainty analyses on COAF).
Finally, selected midpoint results were compared with those from previous studies.
53: The following comparisons among impact assessment indica- tors corresponding to the various modelled ROAF and COAF, consolidated into a single score, were performed:

<list>The main (in terms of annual quantities produced) amend- ments, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic fertilisers, be it ROAF or COAF, were grouped and compared per t N, P, K, and C in fresh mass. 
The main organic amendments and fertilisers were com- pared, across groups, in terms of their IOMS, MFE, and KeqN, the former being an indicator of the amending val- ue of ROAF/COAF and the two latter being indicators of N "quality" (which tend to converge for most organic amendments and fertilisers). These indicators are useful devices to compare the agronomic relevance of ROAF and COAF. IOMS2 stands for "index of organic matter stability", representing the proportion of organic matter that will likely contribute to the replenishment of C stocks in soil (i.e. the percentage of organic matter in the added product estimated to be present in the soil approximately 1 year after application). It is computed from the biochem- ical soluble, cellulosic, and lignin-like fractions of organic matter in ROAF/COAF and the proportion of rapidly</list>
2 Referred to among French institutions and researchers as "indice de stabilité de la matière organique (ISMO)".
Fig. 4 Contribution analysis of the main substrates used in organic fertilisers produced in France, corresponding to the single score per 1 t fresh matter

mineralisable C. Details and calculated values are provided in Lashermes et al. (2009) and Fuchs et al. (2014). MFE stands for "mineral fertiliser equivalent", a measure of the capability of organic fertilisers to substitute mineral ones, based on their content of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N-i.e. readily available for the plants-(Brockmann et al. 2018). It conveys the residual fertilising value of organic fertilisers after NO3 and NH3 emissions. It is calculated as "first year" and "long term", based on short- and long-term N mineralisation rates (thus, the latter includes delayed release of mineralised N). MFE calculation means and data are provided in Brockmann et al. (2018). KeqN (of the crop cycle) represents a coef- ficient of N fertiliser equivalence (COMIFER 2013), representing the ratio between the amount of N provided

<list>by a mineral fertiliser of the ammonium nitrate type and the total amount of N provided by an organic fertiliser which allows the same N absorption by the crop over its cycle. It depends on the type of crop and its conditions (rotation, seasonality). KeqN details and calculated values are provided in COMIFER (2013). It is assumed that the mineralisation of N converges in the long term towards that of soil humus (soil organic matter).

1: <header>3.1 Life cycle inventories </header>

The main substrates, treatment routes, and final ROAF and COAF modelled are depicted in Fig. 2 and detailed in the Supplementary Material (section 1). These routes are flexible, as, in practice, the intermediate steps can be performed in an alternative order, some steps omitted, or steps from different treatment chains combined in alternative ways. It was attempted to represent the most common pathways, and final ROAF often include (unless indicated otherwise) steps repre- sented as an average of technologies. For instance, liquid sew- age sludge is thickened from 1% DM to up to 7% DM (mean 4.2% DM) by flotation, by gravity without or by dewatering table with chemical conditioning.

Key characteristics of modelled ROAF and COAF, of agro- nomic relevance, are presented in Appendix Table 5. Their con- tents of trace metal element contamination are presented in Avadí et al. (2020), as well as uncertainty data for their nutrient values.

Their classification as amendment or fertiliser is based on the criteria presented in Avadí et al. (2020) and Table 2.

To allow for the combination of individual treatment steps into treatment routes/pathways, no cumulative im- pacts are included in individual treatment step processes, such as the successive sludge dry matter concentration steps (thickening, stabilisation, dewatering, and drying). Treatment routes were modelled by combining individual treatment steps.

Typical routes, by which the majority of organic res- idues are treated, are shown in the Supplementary Material (section 1). LCIs for the main steps across treat- ment routes are presented in Avadí et al. (2020), aggre- gated per dominant substrate. The full list of LCIs, in- cluding uncertainty data, is to be implemented in the AGRIBALYSE database in 2020.

Few infrastructure data were available; thus, they were ap- proximated from suitable proxies, such as adapting infrastruc- ture data of similar technologies and rescaling AGRIBALYSE and ecoinvent infrastructure processes (e.g. anaerobic diges- tion plant). Further updates to these data are in progress, by including primary data compiled by various recent French research projects focused on organic waste treatments (e.g. Oudart 2013; https://diva.irstea.fr/.

Their classification as amendment or fertiliser is based on the criteria presented in Avadí et al. (2020) and Table 2.

To allow for the combination of individual treatment steps into treatment routes/pathways, no cumulative im- pacts are included in individual treatment step processes, such as the successive sludge dry matter concentration steps (thickening, stabilisation, dewatering, and drying). Treatment routes were modelled by combining individual treatment steps.

Typical routes, by which the majority of organic res- idues are treated, are shown in the Supplementary Material (section 1). LCIs for the main steps across treat- ment routes are presented in Avadí et al. (2020), aggre- gated per dominant substrate. The full list of LCIs, in- cluding uncertainty data, is to be implemented in the AGRIBALYSE database in 2020.

Few infrastructure data were available; thus, they were ap- proximated from suitable proxies, such as adapting infrastruc- ture data of similar technologies and rescaling AGRIBALYSE and ecoinvent infrastructure processes (e.g. anaerobic diges- tion plant). Further updates to these data are in progress, by including primary data compiled by various recent French research projects focused on organic waste treatments (e.g. Oudart 2013; https://diva.irstea.fr/.

1: <header>3.2.1 Relative impacts of producing different organic amendments and fertilisers </header>

The impacts for all ROAF and COAF, classified as organic amendments, were computed. The resulting single scores, per t fresh mass and per N, P, or K in fresh mass, as well as other detailed endpoint and midpoint results, are presented in the Supplementary Material (sections 2 and 3).

The aggregated impacts of these ROAF differ by up to three orders of magnitude per t of fresh mass product or of each nutrient. In principle, amendments based on the dewatering or phase separation of substrates, feature rela- tively higher impacts, due to energy expenditures. The exceptions are limed and dewatered sludge-based amend- ments, despite their consumption of energy and reactants, because digestates and composts feature higher energy expenditures.

Untreated livestock effluents feature the lowest rela- tive impacts for all functional units. Compost-based com- mercial organic amendments feature lower impacts than other composts, both per t of fresh mass and of nutrients or C, due to the inclusion of additional nutrient-rich inputs.

A comparison of single scores for various ROAF and COAF, classified as organic fertilisers, shows that, in general, the impact intensity of commercial organo- mineral fertilisers and products based on liquid fractions of slurry or digestates, features impacts higher than those of untreated livestock effluents, raw digestates, commercial organic fertilisers, and sludge-based prod- ucts without drying (Fig. 3). The modelled COAF con- sist of manure-based composts enriched with additional organic and synthetic inputs. COAF have a higher con- tent of N, P and K than treated sludge (Appendix Table 5), thus featuring relatively lower impacts than per t of nutrients.

Rendered animal products, animal effluents, and raw digestates feature slightly lower impacts per t fresh mass than COAF, yet the latter have lower impacts per t of nutrients than some digestates. Manure-based digestates feature the lowest impacts per all functional units, due to the initial nutrient load, concentrated by the anaerobic digestion treatment. The one order of magnitude

difference between poultry and cattle slurry is due to their relative emissions during storage: NH3 and N20 emissions from poultry manure are roughly 300% higher, despite similar emission factors (expressed as % NH3-N or N2O-N of initial N) for both manures, due to the differences in initial N content (poultry ~ 18 kg/t, cattle ~6 kg/t).

Thickened sludge features relatively low impacts be- cause thickening is based on not SO energy-intensive technologies (i.e. gravitation flotation, dewatering table). The dry matter contents in sludge dewatered by press

filter (33% DM, 25% of all dewatered sludge), and its associated impacts, are higher than those in sludge dewatered by centrifugation (22.5% DM, 66% of all dewatered sludge), despite the higher energy consump- tion by the latter, due to intensive use of reactants by the former. That is the reason why the impacts of the average dewatered t of sludge (a weighted average of technologies, yielding 25% DM) are higher than those of the average t of sludge dewatered by centrifugation. In general, the impacts of treated sludge increase one order of magnitude per additional treatment step.

The C content of ROAF/COAF, retained here as a proxy of their amendment value, can be refined by ex- pressing it in terms of IOMS. Similarly, the delivery of N by ROAF/COAF can be refined by expressing it in terms of MFE and KeqN. A comparison of the impacts per t IOMS, MFE, and KeqN and those per t C and N, for all ROAF and COAF, is shown in the Supplementary Material (sections 3.1 and 3.2).

The main differences between C and IOMS correspond to products featuring an IOMS lower than 50% (commer- cial fertilisers, sludge-based products, and certain live- stock effluents). Untreated and digested livestock efflu- ents, animal meals, and commercial organic fertilisers fea- ture the lowest impacts, suggesting a correlation between their superior environmental performance and their amendment value. This observation partially supports the argument of COAF producers that their products contrib- ute to improving soil quality.

Products featuring the higher contents of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N (e.g. raw and digested slur- ries) show shorter distances between impacts based on MFE/KeqN and on total N. The differences among these two sets of impacts reach up to one order of magnitude.

The provision of maize silage is very dominant in all treat- ments that include it as input, as compared with other sub- strates. The agricultural phase dominates the impacts of maize silage and processed agricultural residues using co-products with commercial value. Energy, direct emissions from stock- ing, and transport are other key drivers of impacts across sub- strates (Fig. 4).

A contribution analysis (Fig. 5) of selected treatments yielding amendments and fertilisers indicated that no sin- gle inventory item is the main contributor for all treat- ments, but rather specific items tend to dominate types of treatments. For instance, energy, chemicals (i.e. reac- tants), and direct emissions dominated impacts for sew- age sludge treatments. Impacts of untreated stocked live- stock effluents are dominated by direct emissions. Transport and energy dominate the impacts of most com- posts and digestates. Commercial amendments and fertilisers are dominated by the provision of processed agro-industrial residues, while mineral inputs dominate impacts of organo-mineral fertilisers.

When organic and organo-mineral fertilisers are com- pared with the most common mineral fertilisers (as modelled in ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE) per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O (Fig. 6), only a few feature impacts within the same orders of magnitude of those corresponding to mineral fertilisers. Only untreated poultry manure and some digestates feature impacts lower than those of N mineral fertilisers, while commercial or- ganic fertilisers feature impacts one order of magnitude

higher. K-fertilisers from ammonia stripping of slurry and agricultural digestate feature higher impacts per t K2O. A ranking of all ROAF, COAF, and mineral fertilisers, based on their impacts per t fresh mass, N, P, K, and C, is included in the Supplementary Material (section 5.4). A subset of this ranking (Table 3) shows that untreated and digested livestock effluents are system- atically among the top 10 products featuring the lower impacts, that commercial organic fertilisers are relatively higher impacting sources of C, and that mineral P and K fertilisers compare favourably, in terms of impacts, with organic products as sources of these nutrients.

Statistical treatment of the results per t of wet mass (Supplementary Material, section 5) shows that the im- pacts of mineral fertilisers are significantly different from those of amendments and fertilisers, including the corre- sponding range of impacts of each category. Moreover, pairwise comparisons show that significant differences exist between mineral fertilisers and commercial organic fertilisers, composts, and digestates (agricultural or not), as well as treated and untreated livestock effluents and sewage sludge.

If the impact categories climate change and resource depletion are taken in isolation (Supplementary Material, section 3.5), the differences in environmental burden of delivering an equivalent amount of nutrients between mineral and organic fertilisers are kept, except for the resource depletion impacts of poultry manure, which get reduced by two orders of magnitude (considering that no impacts of poultry production are assigned to manure).

Organic and organo-mineral fertilisers were compared with mineral fertilisers per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O, on the basis of the fertilisation needs of 1 ha of wheat (Fig. 7), without combining ROAF/COAF in the fertilisation regime. The amounts of products rep- resent the minimum quantity of each fertiliser required to fulfil all required nutrient (N-P-K) needs. It was found that only poultry manure, digestates, and the 4-3-5 and 7-6-8 COAF feature lower impacts than the alternative mineral fertilisation regime, while other COAF feature slightly higher impacts. Most COAF other than the organo-mineral ones feature impacts similar or lower than those of mineral fertilisers, per t total N, P2O5, and K2O, but higher per t MFE/KeqN.

In general, to fully or partially replace mineral fertilisation, larger amounts of organic products would have to be applied. Nonetheless, as amply described in the literature, repeated application of ROAF may improve in the long term the general fertility and health (e.g. its quality) of agricultural soils (e.g. Odlare et al. 2011; Martínez-Blanco et al. 2013; Insam et al. 2015) while also representing a higher risk of direct emissions and

accumulation in soil of persistent contaminants (e.g. Evanylo et al. 2008; Lopes et al. 2011; Nkoa 2014; Liu 2016). Moreover, in the soil, organic fertilisers behave differently than mineral fertilisers, as they contain for in- stance an important proportion of organic N, a portion of which quickly mineralises, but some of which takes time to become available. The calculation of meaningful min- eral fertiliser equivalents for organic fertilisers is a com- plex endeavour (Stedje et al. 2018), affected by various factors such as the application method (Brockmann et al. 2018). At any rate, the return of organic residues to agri- cultural soils is considered a positive step towards more sustainable agriculture (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Brockmann et al. 2018), yet requiring proper management.

Under the assumption of a regional assessment con- text, the impacts of the production of organic fertilisers required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat with an alternative system were compared. The alternative system consisted of (a) producing an equivalent amount of mineral fertiliser, and (b) otherwise disposing of the amount of organic waste required to produce the equivalent amount (in terms of total N, K2O, and P2O5) of organic fertilisers. Organic waste treatment/disposal processes available in ecoinvent were selected for said comparison (Fig. 8). The impacts of producing organic fertilisers are considerably lower than those of the alternative system for poultry manure and biowaste-based digestate, but higher for dried sewage sludge compared with mineral fertilisation + sludge incineration (due to the low K con- tent of dried sludge coupled with the high energy re- quirements and consumption of reactants of its treatment).

The rather simplistic comparison in Fig. 8 ignores the im- pacts of direct field emissions associated with fertilisation and a functional unit capturing all functions of ROAF (provision of nutrients, soil amelioration, organic waste disposal, etc.). Moreover, few alternative organic waste treatments are avail- able in the ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE databases.

difference between poultry and cattle slurry is due to their relative emissions during storage: NH3 and N20 emissions from poultry manure are roughly 300% higher, despite similar emission factors (expressed as % NH3-N or N2O-N of initial N) for both manures, due to the differences in initial N content (poultry ~ 18 kg/t, cattle ~6 kg/t).
