filter (33% DM, 25% of all dewatered sludge), and its associated impacts, are higher than those in sludge dewatered by centrifugation (22.5% DM, 66% of all dewatered sludge), despite the higher energy consump- tion by the latter, due to intensive use of reactants by the former. That is the reason why the impacts of the average dewatered t of sludge (a weighted average of technologies, yielding 25% DM) are higher than those of the average t of sludge dewatered by centrifugation. In general, the impacts of treated sludge increase one order of magnitude per additional treatment step.

The C content of ROAF/COAF, retained here as a proxy of their amendment value, can be refined by ex- pressing it in terms of IOMS. Similarly, the delivery of N by ROAF/COAF can be refined by expressing it in terms of MFE and KeqN. A comparison of the impacts per t IOMS, MFE, and KeqN and those per t C and N, for all ROAF and COAF, is shown in the Supplementary Material (sections 3.1 and 3.2).

The main differences between C and IOMS correspond to products featuring an IOMS lower than 50% (commer- cial fertilisers, sludge-based products, and certain live- stock effluents). Untreated and digested livestock efflu- ents, animal meals, and commercial organic fertilisers fea- ture the lowest impacts, suggesting a correlation between their superior environmental performance and their amendment value. This observation partially supports the argument of COAF producers that their products contrib- ute to improving soil quality.

Products featuring the higher contents of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N (e.g. raw and digested slur- ries) show shorter distances between impacts based on MFE/KeqN and on total N. The differences among these two sets of impacts reach up to one order of magnitude.

The provision of maize silage is very dominant in all treat- ments that include it as input, as compared with other sub- strates. The agricultural phase dominates the impacts of maize silage and processed agricultural residues using co-products with commercial value. Energy, direct emissions from stock- ing, and transport are other key drivers of impacts across sub- strates (Fig. 4).

A contribution analysis (Fig. 5) of selected treatments yielding amendments and fertilisers indicated that no sin- gle inventory item is the main contributor for all treat- ments, but rather specific items tend to dominate types of treatments. For instance, energy, chemicals (i.e. reac- tants), and direct emissions dominated impacts for sew- age sludge treatments. Impacts of untreated stocked live- stock effluents are dominated by direct emissions. Transport and energy dominate the impacts of most com- posts and digestates. Commercial amendments and fertilisers are dominated by the provision of processed agro-industrial residues, while mineral inputs dominate impacts of organo-mineral fertilisers.

When organic and organo-mineral fertilisers are com- pared with the most common mineral fertilisers (as modelled in ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE) per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O (Fig. 6), only a few feature impacts within the same orders of magnitude of those corresponding to mineral fertilisers. Only untreated poultry manure and some digestates feature impacts lower than those of N mineral fertilisers, while commercial or- ganic fertilisers feature impacts one order of magnitude

higher. K-fertilisers from ammonia stripping of slurry and agricultural digestate feature higher impacts per t K2O. A ranking of all ROAF, COAF, and mineral fertilisers, based on their impacts per t fresh mass, N, P, K, and C, is included in the Supplementary Material (section 5.4). A subset of this ranking (Table 3) shows that untreated and digested livestock effluents are system- atically among the top 10 products featuring the lower impacts, that commercial organic fertilisers are relatively higher impacting sources of C, and that mineral P and K fertilisers compare favourably, in terms of impacts, with organic products as sources of these nutrients.

Statistical treatment of the results per t of wet mass (Supplementary Material, section 5) shows that the im- pacts of mineral fertilisers are significantly different from those of amendments and fertilisers, including the corre- sponding range of impacts of each category. Moreover, pairwise comparisons show that significant differences exist between mineral fertilisers and commercial organic fertilisers, composts, and digestates (agricultural or not), as well as treated and untreated livestock effluents and sewage sludge.

If the impact categories climate change and resource depletion are taken in isolation (Supplementary Material, section 3.5), the differences in environmental burden of delivering an equivalent amount of nutrients between mineral and organic fertilisers are kept, except for the resource depletion impacts of poultry manure, which get reduced by two orders of magnitude (considering that no impacts of poultry production are assigned to manure).

Organic and organo-mineral fertilisers were compared with mineral fertilisers per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O, on the basis of the fertilisation needs of 1 ha of wheat (Fig. 7), without combining ROAF/COAF in the fertilisation regime. The amounts of products rep- resent the minimum quantity of each fertiliser required to fulfil all required nutrient (N-P-K) needs. It was found that only poultry manure, digestates, and the 4-3-5 and 7-6-8 COAF feature lower impacts than the alternative mineral fertilisation regime, while other COAF feature slightly higher impacts. Most COAF other than the organo-mineral ones feature impacts similar or lower than those of mineral fertilisers, per t total N, P2O5, and K2O, but higher per t MFE/KeqN.

In general, to fully or partially replace mineral fertilisation, larger amounts of organic products would have to be applied. Nonetheless, as amply described in the literature, repeated application of ROAF may improve in the long term the general fertility and health (e.g. its quality) of agricultural soils (e.g. Odlare et al. 2011; Martínez-Blanco et al. 2013; Insam et al. 2015) while also representing a higher risk of direct emissions and

accumulation in soil of persistent contaminants (e.g. Evanylo et al. 2008; Lopes et al. 2011; Nkoa 2014; Liu 2016). Moreover, in the soil, organic fertilisers behave differently than mineral fertilisers, as they contain for in- stance an important proportion of organic N, a portion of which quickly mineralises, but some of which takes time to become available. The calculation of meaningful min- eral fertiliser equivalents for organic fertilisers is a com- plex endeavour (Stedje et al. 2018), affected by various factors such as the application method (Brockmann et al. 2018). At any rate, the return of organic residues to agri- cultural soils is considered a positive step towards more sustainable agriculture (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Brockmann et al. 2018), yet requiring proper management.

Under the assumption of a regional assessment con- text, the impacts of the production of organic fertilisers required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat with an alternative system were compared. The alternative system consisted of (a) producing an equivalent amount of mineral fertiliser, and (b) otherwise disposing of the amount of organic waste required to produce the equivalent amount (in terms of total N, K2O, and P2O5) of organic fertilisers. Organic waste treatment/disposal processes available in ecoinvent were selected for said comparison (Fig. 8). The impacts of producing organic fertilisers are considerably lower than those of the alternative system for poultry manure and biowaste-based digestate, but higher for dried sewage sludge compared with mineral fertilisation + sludge incineration (due to the low K con- tent of dried sludge coupled with the high energy re- quirements and consumption of reactants of its treatment).

The rather simplistic comparison in Fig. 8 ignores the im- pacts of direct field emissions associated with fertilisation and a functional unit capturing all functions of ROAF (provision of nutrients, soil amelioration, organic waste disposal, etc.). Moreover, few alternative organic waste treatments are avail- able in the ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE databases.
4: <header>3.2.1 Relative impacts of producing different organic amendments and fertilisers </header>

The impacts for all ROAF and COAF, classified as organic amendments, were computed. The resulting single scores, per t fresh mass and per N, P, or K in fresh mass, as well as other detailed endpoint and midpoint results, are presented in the Supplementary Material (sections 2 and 3).

The aggregated impacts of these ROAF differ by up to three orders of magnitude per t of fresh mass product or of each nutrient. In principle, amendments based on the dewatering or phase separation of substrates, feature rela- tively higher impacts, due to energy expenditures. The exceptions are limed and dewatered sludge-based amend- ments, despite their consumption of energy and reactants, because digestates and composts feature higher energy expenditures.

Untreated livestock effluents feature the lowest rela- tive impacts for all functional units. Compost-based com- mercial organic amendments feature lower impacts than other composts, both per t of fresh mass and of nutrients or C, due to the inclusion of additional nutrient-rich inputs.

A comparison of single scores for various ROAF and COAF, classified as organic fertilisers, shows that, in general, the impact intensity of commercial organo- mineral fertilisers and products based on liquid fractions of slurry or digestates, features impacts higher than those of untreated livestock effluents, raw digestates, commercial organic fertilisers, and sludge-based prod- ucts without drying (Fig. 3). The modelled COAF con- sist of manure-based composts enriched with additional organic and synthetic inputs. COAF have a higher con- tent of N, P and K than treated sludge (Appendix Table 5), thus featuring relatively lower impacts than per t of nutrients.

Rendered animal products, animal effluents, and raw digestates feature slightly lower impacts per t fresh mass than COAF, yet the latter have lower impacts per t of nutrients than some digestates. Manure-based digestates feature the lowest impacts per all functional units, due to the initial nutrient load, concentrated by the anaerobic digestion treatment. The one order of magnitude

The alternative system consisted of (a) producing an equivalent amount of mineral fertiliser, and (b) otherwise disposing of the amount of organic waste required to produce the equivalent amount (in terms of total N, K2O, and P2O5) of organic fertilisers. Organic waste treatment/disposal processes available in ecoinvent were selected for said comparison (Fig. 8). The impacts of producing organic fertilisers are considerably lower than those of the alternative system for poultry manure and biowaste-based digestate, but higher for dried sewage sludge compared with mineral fertilisation + sludge incineration (due to the low K con- tent of dried sludge coupled with the high energy re- quirements and consumption of reactants of its treatment).

The rather simplistic comparison in Fig. 8 ignores the im- pacts of direct field emissions associated with fertilisation and a functional unit capturing all functions of ROAF (provision of nutrients, soil amelioration, organic waste disposal, etc.). Moreover, few alternative organic waste treatments are avail- able in the ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE databases.
6: <header>3.2.1 Relative impacts of producing different organic amendments and fertilisers </header>

The impacts for all ROAF and COAF, classified as organic amendments, were computed. The resulting single scores, per t fresh mass and per N, P, or K in fresh mass, as well as other detailed endpoint and midpoint results, are presented in the Supplementary Material (sections 2 and 3).

The aggregated impacts of these ROAF differ by up to three orders of magnitude per t of fresh mass product or of each nutrient. In principle, amendments based on the dewatering or phase separation of substrates, feature rela- tively higher impacts, due to energy expenditures. The exceptions are limed and dewatered sludge-based amend- ments, despite their consumption of energy and reactants, because digestates and composts feature higher energy expenditures.

Untreated livestock effluents feature the lowest rela- tive impacts for all functional units. Compost-based com- mercial organic amendments feature lower impacts than other composts, both per t of fresh mass and of nutrients or C, due to the inclusion of additional nutrient-rich inputs.

A comparison of single scores for various ROAF and COAF, classified as organic fertilisers, shows that, in general, the impact intensity of commercial organo- mineral fertilisers and products based on liquid fractions of slurry or digestates, features impacts higher than those of untreated livestock effluents, raw digestates, commercial organic fertilisers, and sludge-based prod- ucts without drying (Fig. 3). The modelled COAF con- sist of manure-based composts enriched with additional organic and synthetic inputs. COAF have a higher con- tent of N, P and K than treated sludge (Appendix Table 5), thus featuring relatively lower impacts than per t of nutrients.

Rendered animal products, animal effluents, and raw digestates feature slightly lower impacts per t fresh mass than COAF, yet the latter have lower impacts per t of nutrients than some digestates. Manure-based digestates feature the lowest impacts per all functional units, due to the initial nutrient load, concentrated by the anaerobic digestion treatment. The one order of magnitude

difference between poultry and cattle slurry is due to their relative emissions during storage: NH3 and N20 emissions from poultry manure are roughly 300% higher, despite similar emission factors (expressed as % NH3-N or N2O-N of initial N) for both manures, due to the differences in initial N content (poultry ~ 18 kg/t, cattle ~6 kg/t).

Thickened sludge features relatively low impacts be- cause thickening is based on not SO energy-intensive technologies (i.e. gravitation flotation, dewatering table). The dry matter contents in sludge dewatered by press

filter (33% DM, 25% of all dewatered sludge), and its associated impacts, are higher than those in sludge dewatered by centrifugation (22.5% DM, 66% of all dewatered sludge), despite the higher energy consump- tion by the latter, due to intensive use of reactants by the former. That is the reason why the impacts of the average dewatered t of sludge (a weighted average of technologies, yielding 25% DM) are higher than those of the average t of sludge dewatered by centrifugation. In general, the impacts of treated sludge increase one order of magnitude per additional treatment step.

The C content of ROAF/COAF, retained here as a proxy of their amendment value, can be refined by ex- pressing it in terms of IOMS. Similarly, the delivery of N by ROAF/COAF can be refined by expressing it in terms of MFE and KeqN. A comparison of the impacts per t IOMS, MFE, and KeqN and those per t C and N, for all ROAF and COAF, is shown in the Supplementary Material (sections 3.1 and 3.2).

The main differences between C and IOMS correspond to products featuring an IOMS lower than 50% (commer- cial fertilisers, sludge-based products, and certain live- stock effluents). Untreated and digested livestock efflu- ents, animal meals, and commercial organic fertilisers fea- ture the lowest impacts, suggesting a correlation between their superior environmental performance and their amendment value. This observation partially supports the argument of COAF producers that their products contrib- ute to improving soil quality.

Products featuring the higher contents of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N (e.g. raw and digested slur- ries) show shorter distances between impacts based on MFE/KeqN and on total N. The differences among these two sets of impacts reach up to one order of magnitude.

The provision of maize silage is very dominant in all treat- ments that include it as input, as compared with other sub- strates. The agricultural phase dominates the impacts of maize silage and processed agricultural residues using co-products with commercial value. Energy, direct emissions from stock- ing, and transport are other key drivers of impacts across sub- strates (Fig. 4).

A contribution analysis (Fig. 5) of selected treatments yielding amendments and fertilisers indicated that no sin- gle inventory item is the main contributor for all treat- ments, but rather specific items tend to dominate types of treatments. For instance, energy, chemicals (i.e. reac- tants), and direct emissions dominated impacts for sew- age sludge treatments. Impacts of untreated stocked live- stock effluents are dominated by direct emissions. Transport and energy dominate the impacts of most com- posts and digestates. Commercial amendments and fertilisers are dominated by the provision of processed agro-industrial residues, while mineral inputs dominate impacts of organo-mineral fertilisers.

When organic and organo-mineral fertilisers are com- pared with the most common mineral fertilisers (as modelled in ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE) per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O (Fig. 6), only a few feature impacts within the same orders of magnitude of those corresponding to mineral fertilisers. Only untreated poultry manure and some digestates feature impacts lower than those of N mineral fertilisers, while commercial or- ganic fertilisers feature impacts one order of magnitude

higher. K-fertilisers from ammonia stripping of slurry and agricultural digestate feature higher impacts per t K2O. A ranking of all ROAF, COAF, and mineral fertilisers, based on their impacts per t fresh mass, N, P, K, and C, is included in the Supplementary Material (section 5.4). A subset of this ranking (Table 3) shows that untreated and digested livestock effluents are system- atically among the top 10 products featuring the lower impacts, that commercial organic fertilisers are relatively higher impacting sources of C, and that mineral P and K fertilisers compare favourably, in terms of impacts, with organic products as sources of these nutrients.

Statistical treatment of the results per t of wet mass (Supplementary Material, section 5) shows that the im- pacts of mineral fertilisers are significantly different from those of amendments and fertilisers, including the corre- sponding range of impacts of each category. Moreover, pairwise comparisons show that significant differences exist between mineral fertilisers and commercial organic fertilisers, composts, and digestates (agricultural or not), as well as treated and untreated livestock effluents and sewage sludge.

If the impact categories climate change and resource depletion are taken in isolation (Supplementary Material, section 3.5), the differences in environmental burden of delivering an equivalent amount of nutrients between mineral and organic fertilisers are kept, except for the resource depletion impacts of poultry manure, which get reduced by two orders of magnitude (considering that no impacts of poultry production are assigned to manure).

Organic and organo-mineral fertilisers were compared with mineral fertilisers per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O, on the basis of the fertilisation needs of 1 ha of wheat (Fig. 7), without combining ROAF/COAF in the fertilisation regime. The amounts of products rep- resent the minimum quantity of each fertiliser required to fulfil all required nutrient (N-P-K) needs. It was found that only poultry manure, digestates, and the 4-3-5 and 7-6-8 COAF feature lower impacts than the alternative mineral fertilisation regime, while other COAF feature slightly higher impacts. Most COAF other than the organo-mineral ones feature impacts similar or lower than those of mineral fertilisers, per t total N, P2O5, and K2O, but higher per t MFE/KeqN.

In general, to fully or partially replace mineral fertilisation, larger amounts of organic products would have to be applied. Nonetheless, as amply described in the literature, repeated application of ROAF may improve in the long term the general fertility and health (e.g. its quality) of agricultural soils (e.g. Odlare et al. 2011; Martínez-Blanco et al. 2013; Insam et al. 2015) while also representing a higher risk of direct emissions and

accumulation in soil of persistent contaminants (e.g. Evanylo et al. 2008; Lopes et al. 2011; Nkoa 2014; Liu 2016). Moreover, in the soil, organic fertilisers behave differently than mineral fertilisers, as they contain for in- stance an important proportion of organic N, a portion of which quickly mineralises, but some of which takes time to become available. The calculation of meaningful min- eral fertiliser equivalents for organic fertilisers is a com- plex endeavour (Stedje et al. 2018), affected by various factors such as the application method (Brockmann et al. 2018). At any rate, the return of organic residues to agri- cultural soils is considered a positive step towards more sustainable agriculture (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Brockmann et al. 2018), yet requiring proper management.

Under the assumption of a regional assessment con- text, the impacts of the production of organic fertilisers required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat with an alternative system were compared. The alternative system consisted of (a) producing an equivalent amount of mineral fertiliser, and (b) otherwise disposing of the amount of organic waste required to produce the equivalent amount (in terms of total N, K2O, and P2O5) of organic fertilisers. Organic waste treatment/disposal processes available in ecoinvent were selected for said comparison (Fig. 8). The impacts of producing organic fertilisers are considerably lower than those of the alternative system for poultry manure and biowaste-based digestate, but higher for dried sewage sludge compared with mineral fertilisation + sludge incineration (due to the low K con- tent of dried sludge coupled with the high energy re- quirements and consumption of reactants of its treatment).

The rather simplistic comparison in Fig. 8 ignores the im- pacts of direct field emissions associated with fertilisation and a functional unit capturing all functions of ROAF (provision of nutrients, soil amelioration, organic waste disposal, etc.). Moreover, few alternative organic waste treatments are avail- able in the ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE databases.

The main differences between C and IOMS correspond to products featuring an IOMS lower than 50% (commer- cial fertilisers, sludge-based products, and certain live- stock effluents). Untreated and digested livestock efflu- ents, animal meals, and commercial organic fertilisers fea- ture the lowest impacts, suggesting a correlation between their superior environmental performance and their amendment value. This observation partially supports the argument of COAF producers that their products contrib- ute to improving soil quality.

Products featuring the higher contents of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N (e.g. raw and digested slur- ries) show shorter distances between impacts based on MFE/KeqN and on total N. The differences among these two sets of impacts reach up to one order of magnitude.

The provision of maize silage is very dominant in all treat- ments that include it as input, as compared with other sub- strates. The agricultural phase dominates the impacts of maize silage and processed agricultural residues using co-products with commercial value. Energy, direct emissions from stock- ing, and transport are other key drivers of impacts across sub- strates (Fig. 4).

A contribution analysis (Fig. 5) of selected treatments yielding amendments and fertilisers indicated that no sin- gle inventory item is the main contributor for all treat- ments, but rather specific items tend to dominate types of treatments. For instance, energy, chemicals (i.e. reac- tants), and direct emissions dominated impacts for sew- age sludge treatments. Impacts of untreated stocked live- stock effluents are dominated by direct emissions. Transport and energy dominate the impacts of most com- posts and digestates. Commercial amendments and fertilisers are dominated by the provision of processed agro-industrial residues, while mineral inputs dominate impacts of organo-mineral fertilisers.

When organic and organo-mineral fertilisers are com- pared with the most common mineral fertilisers (as modelled in ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE) per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O (Fig. 6), only a few feature impacts within the same orders of magnitude of those corresponding to mineral fertilisers. Only untreated poultry manure and some digestates feature impacts lower than those of N mineral fertilisers, while commercial or- ganic fertilisers feature impacts one order of magnitude

higher. K-fertilisers from ammonia stripping of slurry and agricultural digestate feature higher impacts per t K2O. A ranking of all ROAF, COAF, and mineral fertilisers, based on their impacts per t fresh mass, N, P, K, and C, is included in the Supplementary Material (section 5.4). A subset of this ranking (Table 3) shows that untreated and digested livestock effluents are system- atically among the top 10 products featuring the lower impacts, that commercial organic fertilisers are relatively higher impacting sources of C, and that mineral P and K fertilisers compare favourably, in terms of impacts, with organic products as sources of these nutrients.

Statistical treatment of the results per t of wet mass (Supplementary Material, section 5) shows that the im- pacts of mineral fertilisers are significantly different from those of amendments and fertilisers, including the corre- sponding range of impacts of each category. Moreover, pairwise comparisons show that significant differences exist between mineral fertilisers and commercial organic fertilisers, composts, and digestates (agricultural or not), as well as treated and untreated livestock effluents and sewage sludge.

If the impact categories climate change and resource depletion are taken in isolation (Supplementary Material, section 3.5), the differences in environmental burden of delivering an equivalent amount of nutrients between mineral and organic fertilisers are kept, except for the resource depletion impacts of poultry manure, which get reduced by two orders of magnitude (considering that no impacts of poultry production are assigned to manure).

Organic and organo-mineral fertilisers were compared with mineral fertilisers per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O, on the basis of the fertilisation needs of 1 ha of wheat (Fig. 7), without combining ROAF/COAF in the fertilisation regime. The amounts of products rep- resent the minimum quantity of each fertiliser required to fulfil all required nutrient (N-P-K) needs. It was found that only poultry manure, digestates, and the 4-3-5 and 7-6-8 COAF feature lower impacts than the alternative mineral fertilisation regime, while other COAF feature slightly higher impacts. Most COAF other than the organo-mineral ones feature impacts similar or lower than those of mineral fertilisers, per t total N, P2O5, and K2O, but higher per t MFE/KeqN.

In general, to fully or partially replace mineral fertilisation, larger amounts of organic products would have to be applied. Nonetheless, as amply described in the literature, repeated application of ROAF may improve in the long term the general fertility and health (e.g. its quality) of agricultural soils (e.g. Odlare et al. 2011; Martínez-Blanco et al. 2013; Insam et al. 2015) while also representing a higher risk of direct emissions and

accumulation in soil of persistent contaminants (e.g. Evanylo et al. 2008; Lopes et al. 2011; Nkoa 2014; Liu 2016). Moreover, in the soil, organic fertilisers behave differently than mineral fertilisers, as they contain for in- stance an important proportion of organic N, a portion of which quickly mineralises, but some of which takes time to become available. The calculation of meaningful min- eral fertiliser equivalents for organic fertilisers is a com- plex endeavour (Stedje et al. 2018), affected by various factors such as the application method (Brockmann et al. 2018). At any rate, the return of organic residues to agri- cultural soils is considered a positive step towards more sustainable agriculture (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Brockmann et al. 2018), yet requiring proper management.

Under the assumption of a regional assessment con- text, the impacts of the production of organic fertilisers required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat with an alternative system were compared. The alternative system consisted of (a) producing an equivalent amount of mineral fertiliser, and (b) otherwise disposing of the amount of organic waste required to produce the equivalent amount (in terms of total N, K2O, and P2O5) of organic fertilisers. Organic waste treatment/disposal processes available in ecoinvent were selected for said comparison (Fig. 8). The impacts of producing organic fertilisers are considerably lower than those of the alternative system for poultry manure and biowaste-based digestate, but higher for dried sewage sludge compared with mineral fertilisation + sludge incineration (due to the low K con- tent of dried sludge coupled with the high energy re- quirements and consumption of reactants of its treatment).

The rather simplistic comparison in Fig. 8 ignores the im- pacts of direct field emissions associated with fertilisation and a functional unit capturing all functions of ROAF (provision of nutrients, soil amelioration, organic waste disposal, etc.). Moreover, few alternative organic waste treatments are avail- able in the ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE databases.
9: <header>3.2.1 Relative impacts of producing different organic amendments and fertilisers </header>

The impacts for all ROAF and COAF, classified as organic amendments, were computed. The resulting single scores, per t fresh mass and per N, P, or K in fresh mass, as well as other detailed endpoint and midpoint results, are presented in the Supplementary Material (sections 2 and 3).

The aggregated impacts of these ROAF differ by up to three orders of magnitude per t of fresh mass product or of each nutrient. In principle, amendments based on the dewatering or phase separation of substrates, feature rela- tively higher impacts, due to energy expenditures. The exceptions are limed and dewatered sludge-based amend- ments, despite their consumption of energy and reactants, because digestates and composts feature higher energy expenditures.

Untreated livestock effluents feature the lowest rela- tive impacts for all functional units. Compost-based com- mercial organic amendments feature lower impacts than other composts, both per t of fresh mass and of nutrients or C, due to the inclusion of additional nutrient-rich inputs.

A comparison of single scores for various ROAF and COAF, classified as organic fertilisers, shows that, in general, the impact intensity of commercial organo- mineral fertilisers and products based on liquid fractions of slurry or digestates, features impacts higher than those of untreated livestock effluents, raw digestates, commercial organic fertilisers, and sludge-based prod- ucts without drying (Fig. 3). The modelled COAF con- sist of manure-based composts enriched with additional organic and synthetic inputs. COAF have a higher con- tent of N, P and K than treated sludge (Appendix Table 5), thus featuring relatively lower impacts than per t of nutrients.

Rendered animal products, animal effluents, and raw digestates feature slightly lower impacts per t fresh mass than COAF, yet the latter have lower impacts per t of nutrients than some digestates. Manure-based digestates feature the lowest impacts per all functional units, due to the initial nutrient load, concentrated by the anaerobic digestion treatment. The one order of magnitude

11: <header>3.2.1 Relative impacts of producing different organic amendments and fertilisers </header>

The impacts for all ROAF and COAF, classified as organic amendments, were computed. The resulting single scores, per t fresh mass and per N, P, or K in fresh mass, as well as other detailed endpoint and midpoint results, are presented in the Supplementary Material (sections 2 and 3).

The aggregated impacts of these ROAF differ by up to three orders of magnitude per t of fresh mass product or of each nutrient. In principle, amendments based on the dewatering or phase separation of substrates, feature rela- tively higher impacts, due to energy expenditures. The exceptions are limed and dewatered sludge-based amend- ments, despite their consumption of energy and reactants, because digestates and composts feature higher energy expenditures.

Untreated livestock effluents feature the lowest rela- tive impacts for all functional units. Compost-based com- mercial organic amendments feature lower impacts than other composts, both per t of fresh mass and of nutrients or C, due to the inclusion of additional nutrient-rich inputs.

A comparison of single scores for various ROAF and COAF, classified as organic fertilisers, shows that, in general, the impact intensity of commercial organo- mineral fertilisers and products based on liquid fractions of slurry or digestates, features impacts higher than those of untreated livestock effluents, raw digestates, commercial organic fertilisers, and sludge-based prod- ucts without drying (Fig. 3). The modelled COAF con- sist of manure-based composts enriched with additional organic and synthetic inputs. COAF have a higher con- tent of N, P and K than treated sludge (Appendix Table 5), thus featuring relatively lower impacts than per t of nutrients.

Rendered animal products, animal effluents, and raw digestates feature slightly lower impacts per t fresh mass than COAF, yet the latter have lower impacts per t of nutrients than some digestates. Manure-based digestates feature the lowest impacts per all functional units, due to the initial nutrient load, concentrated by the anaerobic digestion treatment. The one order of magnitude

difference between poultry and cattle slurry is due to their relative emissions during storage: NH3 and N20 emissions from poultry manure are roughly 300% higher, despite similar emission factors (expressed as % NH3-N or N2O-N of initial N) for both manures, due to the differences in initial N content (poultry ~ 18 kg/t, cattle ~6 kg/t).

Thickened sludge features relatively low impacts be- cause thickening is based on not SO energy-intensive technologies (i.e. gravitation flotation, dewatering table). The dry matter contents in sludge dewatered by press

filter (33% DM, 25% of all dewatered sludge), and its associated impacts, are higher than those in sludge dewatered by centrifugation (22.5% DM, 66% of all dewatered sludge), despite the higher energy consump- tion by the latter, due to intensive use of reactants by the former. That is the reason why the impacts of the average dewatered t of sludge (a weighted average of technologies, yielding 25% DM) are higher than those of the average t of sludge dewatered by centrifugation. In general, the impacts of treated sludge increase one order of magnitude per additional treatment step.

The C content of ROAF/COAF, retained here as a proxy of their amendment value, can be refined by ex- pressing it in terms of IOMS. Similarly, the delivery of N by ROAF/COAF can be refined by expressing it in terms of MFE and KeqN. A comparison of the impacts per t IOMS, MFE, and KeqN and those per t C and N, for all ROAF and COAF, is shown in the Supplementary Material (sections 3.1 and 3.2).

The main differences between C and IOMS correspond to products featuring an IOMS lower than 50% (commer- cial fertilisers, sludge-based products, and certain live- stock effluents). Untreated and digested livestock efflu- ents, animal meals, and commercial organic fertilisers fea- ture the lowest impacts, suggesting a correlation between their superior environmental performance and their amendment value. This observation partially supports the argument of COAF producers that their products contrib- ute to improving soil quality.

Products featuring the higher contents of mineralised and rapidly mineralisable N (e.g. raw and digested slur- ries) show shorter distances between impacts based on MFE/KeqN and on total N. The differences among these two sets of impacts reach up to one order of magnitude.

The provision of maize silage is very dominant in all treat- ments that include it as input, as compared with other sub- strates. The agricultural phase dominates the impacts of maize silage and processed agricultural residues using co-products with commercial value. Energy, direct emissions from stock- ing, and transport are other key drivers of impacts across sub- strates (Fig. 4).

A contribution analysis (Fig. 5) of selected treatments yielding amendments and fertilisers indicated that no sin- gle inventory item is the main contributor for all treat- ments, but rather specific items tend to dominate types of treatments. For instance, energy, chemicals (i.e. reac- tants), and direct emissions dominated impacts for sew- age sludge treatments. Impacts of untreated stocked live- stock effluents are dominated by direct emissions. Transport and energy dominate the impacts of most com- posts and digestates. Commercial amendments and fertilisers are dominated by the provision of processed agro-industrial residues, while mineral inputs dominate impacts of organo-mineral fertilisers.

When organic and organo-mineral fertilisers are com- pared with the most common mineral fertilisers (as modelled in ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE) per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O (Fig. 6), only a few feature impacts within the same orders of magnitude of those corresponding to mineral fertilisers. Only untreated poultry manure and some digestates feature impacts lower than those of N mineral fertilisers, while commercial or- ganic fertilisers feature impacts one order of magnitude

higher. K-fertilisers from ammonia stripping of slurry and agricultural digestate feature higher impacts per t K2O. A ranking of all ROAF, COAF, and mineral fertilisers, based on their impacts per t fresh mass, N, P, K, and C, is included in the Supplementary Material (section 5.4). A subset of this ranking (Table 3) shows that untreated and digested livestock effluents are system- atically among the top 10 products featuring the lower impacts, that commercial organic fertilisers are relatively higher impacting sources of C, and that mineral P and K fertilisers compare favourably, in terms of impacts, with organic products as sources of these nutrients.

Statistical treatment of the results per t of wet mass (Supplementary Material, section 5) shows that the im- pacts of mineral fertilisers are significantly different from those of amendments and fertilisers, including the corre- sponding range of impacts of each category. Moreover, pairwise comparisons show that significant differences exist between mineral fertilisers and commercial organic fertilisers, composts, and digestates (agricultural or not), as well as treated and untreated livestock effluents and sewage sludge.

If the impact categories climate change and resource depletion are taken in isolation (Supplementary Material, section 3.5), the differences in environmental burden of delivering an equivalent amount of nutrients between mineral and organic fertilisers are kept, except for the resource depletion impacts of poultry manure, which get reduced by two orders of magnitude (considering that no impacts of poultry production are assigned to manure).

Organic and organo-mineral fertilisers were compared with mineral fertilisers per t N (total, MFE, and KeqN), P2O5, and K2O, on the basis of the fertilisation needs of 1 ha of wheat (Fig. 7), without combining ROAF/COAF in the fertilisation regime. The amounts of products rep- resent the minimum quantity of each fertiliser required to fulfil all required nutrient (N-P-K) needs. It was found that only poultry manure, digestates, and the 4-3-5 and 7-6-8 COAF feature lower impacts than the alternative mineral fertilisation regime, while other COAF feature slightly higher impacts. Most COAF other than the organo-mineral ones feature impacts similar or lower than those of mineral fertilisers, per t total N, P2O5, and K2O, but higher per t MFE/KeqN.

In general, to fully or partially replace mineral fertilisation, larger amounts of organic products would have to be applied. Nonetheless, as amply described in the literature, repeated application of ROAF may improve in the long term the general fertility and health (e.g. its quality) of agricultural soils (e.g. Odlare et al. 2011; Martínez-Blanco et al. 2013; Insam et al. 2015) while also representing a higher risk of direct emissions and

accumulation in soil of persistent contaminants (e.g. Evanylo et al. 2008; Lopes et al. 2011; Nkoa 2014; Liu 2016). Moreover, in the soil, organic fertilisers behave differently than mineral fertilisers, as they contain for in- stance an important proportion of organic N, a portion of which quickly mineralises, but some of which takes time to become available. The calculation of meaningful min- eral fertiliser equivalents for organic fertilisers is a com- plex endeavour (Stedje et al. 2018), affected by various factors such as the application method (Brockmann et al. 2018). At any rate, the return of organic residues to agri- cultural soils is considered a positive step towards more sustainable agriculture (Wassenaar et al. 2014; Brockmann et al. 2018), yet requiring proper management.

Under the assumption of a regional assessment con- text, the impacts of the production of organic fertilisers required to fertilise 1 ha of wheat with an alternative system were compared. The alternative system consisted of (a) producing an equivalent amount of mineral fertiliser, and (b) otherwise disposing of the amount of organic waste required to produce the equivalent amount (in terms of total N, K2O, and P2O5) of organic fertilisers. Organic waste treatment/disposal processes available in ecoinvent were selected for said comparison (Fig. 8). The impacts of producing organic fertilisers are considerably lower than those of the alternative system for poultry manure and biowaste-based digestate, but higher for dried sewage sludge compared with mineral fertilisation + sludge incineration (due to the low K con- tent of dried sludge coupled with the high energy re- quirements and consumption of reactants of its treatment).

The rather simplistic comparison in Fig. 8 ignores the im- pacts of direct field emissions associated with fertilisation and a functional unit capturing all functions of ROAF (provision of nutrients, soil amelioration, organic waste disposal, etc.). Moreover, few alternative organic waste treatments are avail- able in the ecoinvent and AGRIBALYSE databases.

13: <header>3.2.2 Sensitivity and uncertainty </header>

The modelled treatments are highly uncertain and sensi- tive to energy intensity. For instance, a comparison of sludge products based on the average, lower, and higher reported energy requirements (electricity, heat) demon- strates variations of 24% in average (range 5 to 24%) (Supplementary Material, section 4).

Moreover, results are sensitive to key modelling choices such as assigning no production impacts to agri- cultural residues (livestock effluents, harvest residues). For instance, if only 10% of wheat production impacts are allocated, by mass, to straw, impacts would increase

for various composts from 6 to 229%, according to the proportion of straw in the composted substrate (Supplementary Material, section 4.2). This analysis also highlights the potential effect of proxies and assumptions in agricultural LCA: green waste is considered to include 50% of wheat straw, and both materials originally only include, under the AGRIBALYSE guidelines, the impacts of collection and transport.

In another example, if the wool residues (skirts and scouring sludge) used in the compost that constitutes the basic substrate for COAF are assigned 15% of the impacts from wool skirting and scouring-applying an allocation key proposed in Moreira Cardoso (2013)-the impacts of COAF would be considerably higher, especially in the case of amendments and organic fertilisers (Supplementary Material, section 4.3).

For all organic fertilisers, the Monte Carlo-based propaga- tion of data uncertainty (1000 runs, not shown) indicated that the uncertainty associated with the mean endpoint results, expressed as coefficients of variation, ranges from 56% for average agricultural compost, through 105% for slurry and 125% for average agricultural digestate, to 131% for dewatered sludge. Further treatment steps such as drying, phase separation, and chemical or biological treatment in- crease the said uncertainty.

Direct comparisons of the provision of ROAF/COAF and mineral fertilisers, considering data uncertainty, con- firm the initial comparison in Fig. 7. For instance, a pairwise comparison of impacts for poultry manure (MFE long-term and generic N mineral fertiliser, per the MFE requirements of 1 ha of wheat, and including uncertainty propagation (1000 runs, not shown), was per- formed. Results with a 95% confidence interval suggest that the impacts of the former have a 0% chance of being higher than those of the latter for most impact categories, except for acidification and terrestrial eutrophication (100% both). The single score, representing overall im- pacts, shows a 39% chance for the delivery of nutrients by poultry manure to be higher than those of delivering the same amount of nutrients by mineral N fertiliser. The same comparison between the 5-5-10 COAF (MFE long- term = 0.28) and the generic N mineral fertiliser suggests a 100% chance of the impacts (single score) of the former being higher than those of the latter, both in terms of the single scores and per impact categories.

Results with a 95% confidence interval suggest that the impacts of the former have a 0% chance of being higher than those of the latter for most impact categories, except for acidification and terrestrial eutrophication (100% both). The single score, representing overall im- pacts, shows a 39% chance for the delivery of nutrients by poultry manure to be higher than those of delivering the same amount of nutrients by mineral N fertiliser. The same comparison between the 5-5-10 COAF (MFE long- term = 0.28) and the generic N mineral fertiliser suggests a 100% chance of the impacts (single score) of the former being higher than those of the latter, both in terms of the single scores and per impact categories.
16: <header>3.2.3 Comparison with previous studies </header>

Few LCA studies focused on the treatment of organic residues for the production of ROAF/COAF were found, as for instance, most anaerobic digestion studies focus on biogas production, most composting studies focus on biowaste rather than other substrates, and very few deal

with sludge treatments. Moreover, when LCA results are available for the ROAF of interest, only a few impact categories are consistently considered across studies, no- tably climate change. A comparison between climate change results of this work and a few reference studies is presented in Table 4. Comparisons among LCA studies should be interpreted with great caution, as results are likely based on different modelling choices-see for in- stance the reviews by Yoshida et al. (2013), Skowroñska and Filipek (2014), and Morris et al. (2013)-including system boundaries, allocation strategies, and emission modelling. For instance, the values for the compost of dewatered agricultural digestate derived from Rehl and Müller (2011), despite our corrections to include the im- pacts of dewatering (48 kg CO2/t), are much lower than the values calculated in this work. The main reason is the selection of direct emission factors-Amlinger et al. (2008) and Phong (2012), respectively. Both sets of emission factors are off by one order of magnitude.

18: <header>4 Conclusions </header>

Life cycle inventories of the most relevant organic waste treatments yielding organic amendments and fertilisers in France were constructed. These LCIs can also be consid- ered representative for Europe. They are intended to in- form users of AGRIBALYSE, the agricultural LCI data- base into which they will be integrated. The inventories, moreover, shall inform future organic fertiliser model- ling; including the estimation of their direct emissions post-spreading (based on the detailed composition data accompanying the inventories). As the no-allocation of generation impacts to organic residues is a generalised French LCA practice, and these LCIs follow that as- sumption, they are constructed in such a way that users may connect them with alternative substrate LCIs, if they have preference for allocating impacts to residual sub- strates. The inventories were also modelled in terms of fresh mass, which allows users to estimate direct field emissions more accurately.

The environmental impacts of these LCIs were esti- mated with screening LCA. Differences within a few orders of magnitude were found across results for vari- ous treatment pathways and substrates. Treatment prod- ucts including maize silage as input tend to feature the highest impacts, followed by the industrialised organo- mineral fertilisers and ammonia-stripped slurries and digestates. ROAF of agricultural origin (untreated

livestock effluents) and thickened sludge feature the low- est impacts. Composts and digestates from animal efflu- ents tend to feature relatively low impacts. It was con- firmed that energy provision (electricity, heat, transport and other mobile diesel combustion) and direct process emissions are the main contributors to impacts in organic waste treatments, and that modelling choices such as the allocation of impacts to agricultural by-products, affect results.

The comparative impacts of direct field emissions as- sociated with fertilisation by organic and mineral fertilisers exceed the scope of this work, but it has been reported that ROAF users often over-fertilise, as the nu- trient contents of ROAF are often unknown by their users (Liao et al. 2015; Seufert and Ramankutty 2017). Excess of nutrients, that is to say whatever nutrient input plants cannot absorb, usually contributes to eutrophying emissions. The modelling of direct field emissions and the long-term nutrient delivery associated with organic fertilisation is addressed in Avadí et al. (2020) "Suitability of simple N direct field emissions models to represent contrasting agricultural situations in agricul- tural LCA: review and prospectus". These direct emis- sions, including emissions from spreading, likely differ from those of mineral fertilisers, due to the relative con- tents in (readily available) nutrients, C, variable pedo- climatic and crop-dependent mineralisation rates, and the spreading effort.

More and further detailed primary data are required to im- prove the accuracy and representativeness of organic waste treatment inventories. It would allow creating more represen- tative treatment pathways/typologies (for instance, of custom organic fertilisers or of emerging ways of extracting fertilisers directly from residual matrices), and thus more accurately compare the production impacts of organic amendments and fertilisers.

All amounts are expressed per fresh mass, except for items marked with asterisk, which are expressed per DM. When "slurry" is not identified as swine's or cattle's, it refers to a mix of 56% cattle and 44% swine slurries, based on proportions calculated from Houot et al. (2014). "Manure", unless stated otherwise, consists of 96% cattle manure and 4% poultry manure, based on proportions calculated from Houot et al. (2014). "Sludge" stands for sewage sludge from wastewater treatment plants unless stated otherwise. "N/A" stands for not available. Products in O1, O2, and Q3 have their P and K contents expressed as P2O5 and K2O. In the column Classification, "A" stands for amendment and "F" for fertiliser. Legend for codes (identifiers for treatment routes) is shown in Fig. 2 Sources: a Benoît et al. (2014) and Fuchs et al. (2014), b Dhaouadi (2014), Béline et al. (2004), d García-González et al. (2016), e Hjorth et al. (2010), f Removal rates from Tampio et al. (2016), g Bolzonella et al. (2018), h Vaneeckhaute et al. (2017), Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015), j Akhiar (2017), k Solagro-IRSTEA (2012), 1 Solagro (2004), m Priekulis et al. (2016), n Makádi et al. (2012), SOLAGRO (2001), Technical sheets of commercial fertilisers, q Koch and Salou (2016), ADEME (2011a), IFIP (2017), t Parnaudeau et al. (2004), *Calculated. Mineral fertiliser equivalents (MFE) were calculated using the Excel tool included in Brockmann et al. (2018), assuming application by broadcaster, incorporation within 1 day, and no nitrate content. The coefficients of N fertiliser equivalence (KeqN, mean of reported values) are taken from COMIFER (2013). Indices of organic matter stability (IOMS) are taken from Fuchs et al. (2014) and Bouthier et al. (2014)

"Sludge" stands for sewage sludge from wastewater treatment plants unless stated otherwise. "N/A" stands for not available. All amounts are expressed per fresh mass, except for items marked with asterisk, which are expressed per DM. When "slurry" is not identified as swine's or cattle's, it refers to a mix of 56% cattle and 44% swine slurries, based on proportions calculated from Houot et al. (2014). "Manure", unless stated otherwise, consists of 96% cattle manure and 4% poultry manure, based on proportions calculated from Houot et al. (2014). "Sludge" stands for sewage sludge from wastewater treatment plants unless stated otherwise. "N/A" stands for not available. Products in O1, O2, and Q3 have their P and K contents expressed as P2O5 and K2O. In the column Classification, "A" stands for amendment and "F" for fertiliser. Legend for codes (identifiers for treatment routes) is shown in Fig. 2 Sources: a Benoît et al. (2014) and Fuchs et al. (2014), b Dhaouadi (2014), Béline et al. (2004), d García-González et al. (2016), e Hjorth et al. (2010), f Removal rates from Tampio et al. (2016), g Bolzonella et al. (2018), h Vaneeckhaute et al. (2017), Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015), j Akhiar (2017), k Solagro-IRSTEA (2012), 1 Solagro (2004), m Priekulis et al. (2016), n Makádi et al. (2012), SOLAGRO (2001), Technical sheets of commercial fertilisers, q Koch and Salou (2016), ADEME (2011a), IFIP (2017), t Parnaudeau et al. (2004), *Calculated. Mineral fertiliser equivalents (MFE) were calculated using the Excel tool included in Brockmann et al. (2018), assuming application by broadcaster, incorporation within 1 day, and no nitrate content. The coefficients of N fertiliser equivalence (KeqN, mean of reported values) are taken from COMIFER (2013). Indices of organic matter stability (IOMS) are taken from Fuchs et al. (2014) and Bouthier et al. (2014)

"Sludge" stands for sewage sludge from wastewater treatment plants unless stated otherwise. "N/A" stands for not available. All amounts are expressed per fresh mass, except for items marked with asterisk, which are expressed per DM. When "slurry" is not identified as swine's or cattle's, it refers to a mix of 56% cattle and 44% swine slurries, based on proportions calculated from Houot et al. (2014). "Manure", unless stated otherwise, consists of 96% cattle manure and 4% poultry manure, based on proportions calculated from Houot et al. (2014). "Sludge" stands for sewage sludge from wastewater treatment plants unless stated otherwise. "N/A" stands for not available. Products in O1, O2, and Q3 have their P and K contents expressed as P2O5 and K2O. In the column Classification, "A" stands for amendment and "F" for fertiliser. Legend for codes (identifiers for treatment routes) is shown in Fig. 2 Sources: a Benoît et al. (2014) and Fuchs et al. (2014), b Dhaouadi (2014), Béline et al. (2004), d García-González et al. (2016), e Hjorth et al. (2010), f Removal rates from Tampio et al. (2016), g Bolzonella et al. (2018), h Vaneeckhaute et al. (2017), Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2015), j Akhiar (2017), k Solagro-IRSTEA (2012), 1 Solagro (2004), m Priekulis et al. (2016), n Makádi et al. (2012), SOLAGRO (2001), Technical sheets of commercial fertilisers, q Koch and Salou (2016), ADEME (2011a), IFIP (2017), t Parnaudeau et al. (2004), *Calculated. Mineral fertiliser equivalents (MFE) were calculated using the Excel tool included in Brockmann et al. (2018), assuming application by broadcaster, incorporation within 1 day, and no nitrate content. The coefficients of N fertiliser equivalence (KeqN, mean of reported values) are taken from COMIFER (2013). Indices of organic matter stability (IOMS) are taken from Fuchs et al. (2014) and Bouthier et al. (2014)
